I sat at the kitchen table and drank it, and the coffee was strong and bitter and real, and the morning was ordinary, and the ordinary felt like a gift. I didn't know what would happen next. I didn't know if the fish would come back. I didn't know if the Architects would stay. I didn't know if Shaw's superiors would accept a world where they had to share the deep with something they couldn't control. I didn't know if I would ever hear the hum again, or if it would become something else—something softer, something that lived in the background of the world like the sound of the sea. I didn't know. And that was okay. Because I had heard something. I had been heard. Something vast and alien and ancient had listened to a broken linguist in a stolen boat, and had understood, and had changed. And that was enough. That was more than enough. That was everything. I washed my cup. I fed the cat. I put on my good shoes—the only pair I owned—and I walked to the beach. The sand was back. Not crystalline, not glowing—just sand. The waves were normal. The gulls were back—not many, but a few, crying their sharp, ordinary cries. And on the beach, scattered among the shells and the seaweed, were fish. Not dead fish. Live fish. Small ones, silver, flipping and gasping in the shallows. The first fish I'd seen in weeks. They were struggling—they were out of water, tossed up by the waves—and I knelt and picked one up and held it in my cupped hands, and it was slippery and alive and perfect. I carried it to the water. I knelt at the edge of the surf and opened my hands, and the fish flipped once, twice, and then it was gone—back into the ocean, back into the deep, back into the world. I watched it go. The water was dark and clear and normal. Below the surface, far below, I thought I could see a faint blue glow—the lattices, the Architects, the deep where they slept. But maybe I was imagining it. Maybe it was just the light. I stood up. I wiped my hands on my pants. I looked at the horizon—empty, open, just sky and water—and I took a breath. It was a good breath. It tasted like salt and morning and the future, whatever the future was. I walked home. The town was still empty, but it wouldn't stay empty. People would come back. They always do. They'd come back to the cracked roads and the peeling paint and the smell of the sea, and they'd rebuild, because that's what people do. They break, and they rebuild, and they break again, and they rebuild again, and that's the whole thing. That's the whole life. And in the deep, something vast and ancient and exhausted was resting, and the ocean was holding it, and the sky was ours, and the deep was theirs, and maybe that was the deal. Maybe that was the answer to the question. *Can we share your sky?* Yes. We can share the sky. We can share the deep. We can share the grief, and the hope, and the weight of being alive in a world that is bigger and stranger and more beautiful than we ever imagined. We can share. That's the answer. That's the only answer there ever was. I went home. I sat at the table. I opened my notebook to the last page, and I wrote one final line: *The fish are coming back.* Then I closed the notebook, and I listened to the ocean, and the ocean was quiet, and the hum was a lullaby, and the world was ordinary, and the ordinary was enough. It was everything.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 8 serves as the resolution chapter of this story, and it has genuine strengths alongside significant weaknesses that deserve careful examination. **What Works:** The chapter maintains Aris's voice reasonably well. Her dry, self-deprecating quality comes through in small moments: "I don't know. I go home. I feed my cat. I wait." The physical grounding—Kepler's weight, the tea, the good shoes—is consistent with the character profile and the story's stated intention to live in the body. The notebook entry is a smart structural choice, giving us Aris's own summary in her cramped, observational voice. The relationship between Aris and Priya is handled with appropriate restraint. Priya drapes a blanket, makes tea, checks vitals—she helps in the way a doctor helps, not in a grand emotional way. This is consistent with both characters. **What Fails:** The chapter's most serious problem is that it abandons the plan's core directives at nearly every turn. The plan explicitly states: "Neat resolution. The story ends with Aris changed, not triumphant." This chapter is triumphant. The fish come back. The Architects retreat. Shaw is given a speech to deliver. The ocean heals. Aris sleeps and dreams of warm, clear water. This is the opposite of the intended ending. The plan also states: "Explaining the aliens. We never fully understand the Architects." But here, Aris explains them completely and confidently: "They didn't know they were killing the fish. They didn't know they were hurting us. They were just running." This is delivered as fact, not as Aris's uncertain interpretation. The mystery is dissolved rather than preserved. The prose in the final third collapses into repetitive, self-congratulatory affirmation. "That was enough. That was more than enough. That was everything." This is the kind of escalating declaration that signals a writer reaching for emotional weight they haven't earned through the scene itself. It appears multiple times in different forms: "That's the whole thing. That's the whole life." "That's the answer. That's the only answer there ever was." These are not Aris's dry, self-lacerating voice—they are inspirational poster copy. The dream sequence is the chapter's lowest point. "The water held me. The light guided me." This is generic uplift, and it directly contradicts the plan's stated avoidance of "impressive language about the alien." The plan wanted inadequate human words and failed comparisons; instead we get a warm, clear, light-filled ocean that is the opposite of the drowning metaphor the story was built around. The dialogue with Shaw is expository and on-the-nose. Aris delivers a policy speech: "Tell them the alternative is a war we can't win... Tell them the Architects are refugees, not invaders." This is the kind of competent, articulate advocacy the plan explicitly wanted to avoid—Aris was supposed to be in over her head, scared, not good at this. Here she sounds like a diplomat. The sea glass detail—"poured into the deep for something that needed to learn how to hold broken things gently"—is the chapter's most egregious forced metaphor. It's a gesture toward profundity that doesn't survive scrutiny. Why would sea glass teach an alien to be gentle? The image is decorative, not earned. The ending's repetition of "ordinary" as a positive value is a legitimate idea, but it's stated so many times and so directly that it loses force. "The ordinary felt like a gift." "The ordinary was enough." "It was everything." The chapter tells us how to feel rather than creating the conditions for feeling. The live fish at the end—Aris releasing one back into the ocean—is a competent image, but it's the kind of symbolic gesture that feels assembled rather than discovered. It's too neat, too cinematic, too resolved. **On the Plan's Directives:** The chapter was supposed to be the resolution, but the plan's resolution was meant to be incomplete and ambiguous. The chapter delivers a complete, emotionally resolved, thematically tidy ending that contradicts the plan's stated goals at nearly every point. The plan said "no neat resolution"; this chapter is almost nothing but neat resolution. **Voice Consistency:** Aris's voice holds in the early sections but dissolves in the final third into something more generic and inspirational. The dry, self-lacerating quality disappears entirely by the time she's releasing fish into the ocean and thinking about how the world is "bigger and stranger and more beautiful than we ever imagined." **Overall:** This is competent, readable, emotionally accessible writing that fundamentally misunderstands or ignores its own stated intentions. It resolves what was meant to remain unresolved, explains what was meant to remain mysterious, and reaches for uplift where the plan called for ambiguity. The prose is not bad, but it is frequently amateurish in its repetition and its tendency to state its themes directly. The chapter reads like a first draft that hasn't yet been pushed toward the harder, stranger ending the story deserved. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 9 Emotionally Engaging: 10 Compelling Plot: 8 Coherent: 13 Weak Dialogue: 12 Tell-Don't-Show: 13 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 14 Amateurish: 11 Purple Prose: 12 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 13 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 7 Characters Consistent with Profile: 9 Followed Chapter Plan: 4 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 12

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is an ambitious, largely successful piece of literary science fiction that demonstrates genuine craft. Let me assess it carefully across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The voice is the story's greatest asset. Aris Thorne is rendered with specificity and consistency — the disgraced linguist, the daughter returning to her mother's house, the woman who was right before anyone would listen. The detail of the "drowning" paper that went viral for the wrong reasons is elegant worldbuilding that also serves character. The notebook as a recurring device works well, grounding the reader in Aris's methodical, observational nature. The central conceit — that alien communication would feel like sensation rather than language, specifically grief — is genuinely creative and thematically coherent. The fish-as-receivers idea is inventive and the story commits to it. The Architects as refugees rather than invaders is a meaningful subversion of first-contact tropes. The prose is generally controlled. The author knows when to be spare and when to expand. "The megastructures sat on the horizon like the sentence you can't stop reading" is a strong image. The cat Kepler functions well as a grounding presence without becoming cloying. WEAKNESSES: The piece has a significant problem with repetition of its own best lines. "That was enough. That was all there was." appears multiple times across chapters, as does the drowning metaphor, the "not alone" refrain, and variations on "heavy." What works powerfully once becomes a tic by the third or fourth deployment. This is a real craft failure — the author finds a good note and hammers it until it loses resonance. Shaw is underdeveloped. He functions as a plot mechanism — delivering information, creating urgency — but his characterization is thin. The "plain, forgettable features" description is itself forgettable. His dialogue is largely expository. The forty-eight-hour ultimatum is a genre cliché that the story doesn't interrogate. The transformation in Chapter 7 — where Aris goes to the center of the megastructure and essentially resolves the conflict by sending the Architects an emotional transmission — is somewhat unearned. The story builds carefully toward this moment but the resolution itself happens too quickly and too cleanly. The Architects' response of "Oh" — recognition and immediate behavioral change — compresses what should be a more complex negotiation. The story earns the emotional register but not the plot mechanics. The final two chapters (7 and 8) suffer from diminishing returns. Chapter 8 in particular is largely redundant — it restates themes already expressed, and the final fish-releasing scene, while sweet, is predictable. The story would be stronger ending at the close of Chapter 7. The dialogue is functional but occasionally clunky. Dale's phone conversation in Chapter 1 is a bit too on-the-nose in establishing Aris's isolation. Shaw's dialogue is largely expository delivery. The "forced poetry" problem: the author has a tendency to reach for resonant closing lines that don't always earn their weight. "That was everything" appears multiple times. The final lines of chapters often feel like the author is trying to land a punch that the chapter hasn't fully set up. This is the most consistent weakness — a kind of performed profundity that occasionally tips into purple prose. The Priya character is well-drawn but underused. Her role as the grounded, practical friend who believes Aris is valuable, but she disappears for long stretches and her final scene is brief. The pacing is generally good through the first five chapters, then accelerates too rapidly in the final three. The forty-eight-hour ultimatum compresses what should be a more gradual revelation. OVERALL: This is a genuinely good piece of literary science fiction with a distinctive voice, a creative central conceit, and real emotional intelligence. Its failures are the failures of a talented writer who over-relies on their best moves — the repetition of key phrases, the performed profundity of chapter endings, and a resolution that is emotionally satisfying but mechanically thin. It is not amateurish in the pejorative sense, but it has amateurish habits. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 15 Nuanced Characters: 13 Pacing: 13 World Building: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Coherent: 15 Weak Dialogue: 8 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 8 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 9 Unearned Transformations: 10 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18
Avg Final Item Score: 68.2/100
+ Darkly Comic British YA Fiction — Midnight Blood (Chapter Avg: 76.9 | Final: 76.2)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Darkly Comic British YA Fiction Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: The text came just after midnight: 🩸🩸🩸
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Darkly Comic British YA Fiction Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: The text came just after midnight: 🩸🩸🩸 -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
Of course. Here is a comprehensive writing plan for your darkly comic British YA fiction prompt. *** # Brainstorming Let's cast a wide net and explore the possibilities for this story. **1. The "Blood" & The Sender:** * **Literal Blood:** The emojis could be a threat, a prank involving animal blood, or a genuine (and disgusting) message containing real human blood from a cut or nosebleed. * **Metaphorical Blood:** It could refer to family ("blood is thicker than water"), a blood pact made in the past, a blood oath, or a gang initiation. It might refer to a blood drive, a blood bank, or a medical condition. * **The Sender's Identity:** * **A Prankster Friend:** The sender is a close friend of the protagonist with a morbid sense of humour. The "dark comedy" comes from their escalating, tasteless jokes. * **A Secret Admirer/Enemy:** The sender is someone with a crush or a grudge, communicating in a cryptic, dramatic way. * **A Cry for Help:** The sender is the one in trouble, and the emojis are a shorthand for "I'm hurt" or "I'm in danger." * **A Group/Club:** A secret society at school (e.g., a dramatic society, a goth clique, a historical re-enactment club) that uses it as an initiation signal. * **A Mistake:** A wrong number, intended for someone else entirely. The protagonist gets dragged into someone else's drama. **2. Characters & Setting:** * **Protagonist (Protag):** Cynical, witty, and observant. Probably from a slightly dysfunctional but loving family. They are more likely to be annoyed than scared by the initial text. Their internal monologue provides the "darkly comic" voice. * *Example:* **Leo,** a 16-year-old aspiring screenwriter who sees life in terms of bad movie tropes. His mum is a "wellness influencer" who live-streams her smoothie-making, and his dad is a history teacher obsessed with the local canal system. * **The Best Friend (The Accomplice):** The one who receives the text with Leo. More pragmatic and anxious, acting as a foil to Leo's dramatic flair. They are the "Watson" to Leo's "Holmes," constantly pointing out the flaws in his plans. * *Example:* **Moose** (real name probably Marcus), a lanky, perpetually worried boy who is brilliant at maths but terrible at social interaction. His main concern is not getting a criminal record before his GCSEs. * **The Suspects (A Rogues' Gallery):** * **The New Kid:** An enigmatic figure who arrived mid-term and dresses like a Victorian ghost hunter. * **The Overly-Dramatic Friend:** Part of their friend group, always performing, could have sent it as a "bit." * **The School Bully:** A cliché, but their cruelty could have escalated to psychological warfare. * **The Eccentric Teacher:** The Media Studies teacher who encourages "method acting" in all aspects of life. * **Setting:** A drab, rain-soaked town in the UK. The school is a concrete comprehensive with delusions of grandeur. Key locations: The local all-night diner ("The Greasy Spoon of Despair"), the abandoned Victorian pump house by the canal, the sterile modern library, Leo's cluttered bedroom. **3. Tone & "Darkly Comic" Elements:** * **Tone:** The stakes should feel high to the characters, but the situations they find themselves in are fundamentally mundane and awkward. The comedy comes from the contrast between their perceived "thriller movie" scenario and the pathetic reality. * **Humour Sources:** * **Internal Monologue:** Leo's sarcastic commentary on everything. * **Dialogue:** Snappy, realistic British teen banter. Lots of understatement ("Well, that's sub-optimal"). * **Situational Comedy:** Getting locked in a cupboard while spying, trying to look inconspicuous while covered in mud from the canal bank, having a serious conversation interrupted by a parent asking if they've done their homework. * **Character Quirks:** Moose's detailed contingency plans for every social disaster, Leo's mum trying to "smudge" the house with sage to get rid of "bad vibes." **4. Plot Beats & Pacing (Over 8 Chapters):** * **Possible Story Arc 1 (The Prank Gone Wrong):** 1. **Chapter 1: The Text.** Leo & Moose receive the 🩸🩸🩸. They speculate wildly, decide it's a prank from their friend, and plot revenge. 2. **Chapter 2: The Investigation.** Their "revenge" prank backfires spectacularly, implicating them in a minor crime (like accidentally setting off the school fire alarm). They realise the text wasn't from their friend. 3. **Chapter 3: Suspect Zero.** They create a suspect list. Their attempts to interrogate the New Kid lead to a deeply awkward and public misunderstanding. 4. **Chapter 4: The Trail Goes Cold (and Damp).** They follow a lead to the canal pump house, get soaked, find nothing but a stray cat and some graffiti. 5. **Chapter 5: A Second Message.** A new text arrives: a location pin for the school drama studio. They assume it's a trap. 6. **Chapter 6: The Stakeout.** They hide in the drama studio prop room, only to discover the school's Drama Society is rehearsing a terribly pretentious play about Dracula. The "blood" emojis are part of their promotional campaign. 7. **Chapter 7: Confrontation & Comedy.** They confront the Drama Society president, leading to a ridiculous argument about artistic integrity. Just as they think it's solved, they realise the president didn't send the *first* text. 8. **Chapter 8: The Mundane Truth.** They trace the original message back to a simple, anticlimactic source (e.g., a younger sibling messing with Leo's phone, or a wrong number from a kid in the year below trying to arrange a clandestine meeting for a Dungeons & Dragons game). The story ends with them back in Leo's room, exhausted and mildly traumatised by how boring real mysteries are. * **Possible Story Arc 2 (The Cry for Help):** 1. **Chapter 1: The Text.** The emojis are dismissed as spam or a glitch. 2. **Chapter 2: The Guilt.** Leo can't shake the feeling it's real. Moose provides logical reasons why it's not, but Leo's curiosity (and hero complex) wins. 3. **Chapter 3: Digital Detectives.** They try (and fail) to trace the number, getting bogged down in online forums about burner phones and spy apps. 4. **Chapter 4: The Human Element.** They start asking around casually, which gets them labelled as gossips or weirdos. 5. **Chapter 5: The Pattern.** They connect the emoji message to a series of seemingly unrelated odd events: a student leaving school abruptly, a teacher acting strangely, a blood drive poster being defaced. 6. **Chapter 6: The Wrong Conclusion.** All evidence points to the eccentric teacher. They plan to expose them. 7. **Chapter 7: The Reveal.** Their dramatic "exposure" fails. The teacher reveals they've been acting strange because they're going through a divorce and are trying to win back their spouse with grand, romantic gestures (hence the defaced poster—they were trying to write a love note on it). The student who left was their child. 8. **Chapter 8: The Real Message.** They finally discover the truth: the emojis were sent by the teacher's child, who was having a panic attack about their parents' separation and texted the wrong number in their distress. The story ends with Leo and Moose feeling a mix of relief, embarrassment, and a newfound understanding that real-life drama is rarely as simple as a movie plot. # Reflection Okay, let's think about what we've got here. **What Works:** * **The "British YA" tone is crucial.** Both plot ideas lean into this with the mundane settings, witty dialogue, and low-stakes-but-high-drama feel. The comedy is strongest when it's understated and comes from character, not just wacky situations. Leo's internal monologue as a "bad screenwriter" is a very strong vehicle for this tone. * **The "Darkly Comic" element is best served by subversion.** The audience expects a thriller. The biggest laughs will come from the reveal that the "dark" secret is something profoundly ordinary and awkward. **Story Arc 1 (The Prank)** does this perfectly. The Dracula play reveal is a great comedic set-piece. The final wrong-number reveal is a classic anticlimax that fits the genre. * **Character Dynamics:** The cynical dreamer (Leo) and the anxious pragmatist (Moose) is a proven, effective duo. Their friendship is the heart of the story, and their conflicting approaches to the "mystery" will generate both comedy and genuine emotion. Leo's mum as a live-streaming wellness influencer is a fantastic, modern comic detail that can provide levity and interruption at key moments. * **Pacing for 8 Chapters:** Both arcs fit the chapter count well. They have a clear inciting incident, rising action with complications, a false climax, a real climax, and a resolution. The 1000-word chapter format is perfect for snappy, scene-based storytelling. **What Doesn't Work (or needs refinement):** * **Story Arc 2 (The Cry for Help)** feels a bit more like a straight drama with comedic elements, rather than a "dark comedy." The stakes are higher (a genuine cry for help), which might make the comedic tone feel inappropriate or forced. The reveal about the teacher's divorce is sad, not particularly funny. It risks becoming a "very special episode" rather than a consistently witty story. It leans more "heartwarming" than "darkly comic." * **The "Thriller" Trap:** We have to be careful not to let the plot get too convoluted. If the mystery becomes too complex, the anticlimactic ending will feel like a cheat, not a punchline. The clues and suspects should be somewhat ridiculous from the start. The humour should signal to the reader that this isn't a *real* thriller. * **The Protagonist's Motivation:** In Arc 2, Leo's motivation is guilt and heroism, which is noble. In Arc 1, his motivation is annoyance and a desire for revenge, which is funnier and more fitting for a cynical teen. Arc 1's motivation feels more authentic to the "dark comedy" genre. **Conclusion:** While both ideas have merit, **Story Arc 1 (The Prank Gone Wrong)** feels much stronger for fulfilling the specific "Darkly Comic" prompt. The structure of escalating, failed investigations leading to a series of absurd reveals is a perfect engine for comedy. The final reveal being a mundane wrong number provides the ultimate darkly comic punchline: all that panic, suspicion, and mud-stained drama was for absolutely nothing. It honours the initial ominous prompt while completely subverting it in the most British, understated way possible. The characters and their dynamic are well-suited to this arc. This seems like the most compelling and cohesive direction.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention **Core Concept:** A darkly comic British YA short story where an ominous midnight text (🩸🩸🩸) sends two cynical, witty teens on an escalating investigation that reveals nothing is as dramatic as it seems. The story will subvert thriller/mystery tropes at every turn, with the comedy arising from the yawning gap between the protagonists' perceived "movie plot" scenario and the pathetic, mundane reality. **Plot Direction:** I will follow **Story Arc 1 (The Prank Gone Wrong)**. The structure is: ominous text -> assumption it's a prank from a friend -> failed revenge prank that backfires -> investigation into a series of ridiculous suspects -> escalating stakes (but decreasing competence) -> false climax (the Dracula play reveal) -> anticlimactic real reveal (a wrong number from a younger kid arranging a D&D session). The emotional core is the friendship between Leo and Moose, and the comedy is in their consistent failure to be competent detectives. **Voice & Tone:** * **Narrative Voice:** Close third-person, anchored firmly in Leo's perspective. His internal monologue will be the primary vehicle for the dark comedy—sarcastic, self-aware, and constantly comparing reality to film tropes. * **Dialogue:** Snappy, realistic, full of British understatement and teen vernacular. Conversations will often be interrupted by mundane intrusions (parents, teachers, phone notifications). * **Tone:** The story will maintain a consistent "mock-serious" tone. The prose will sometimes adopt the language of a thriller or detective noir when describing mundane events (e.g., "The rain fell like a million tiny accusations on the stained concrete of the school car park"). * **Humour Sources:** Situational irony, failed grand plans, character quirks (Leo's mum's wellness streaming, Moose's contingency plans), and the sheer awkwardness of teen life. **Stylistic Choices:** * **Chapter Structure:** Each chapter will end on a mini-cliffhanger or moment of decision, propelling the plot forward. * **Pacing:** Fast and scene-based. Each chapter is a distinct "beat" in the investigation (The Text, The Failed Revenge, The Suspect, The Lead, The New Message, The Stakeout, The False Reveal, The Truth). This will keep the 8x1000-word scope tight. * **Setting as Character:** The drab, rain-soaked town will be lovingly detailed in all its mundane glory—the all-night diner, the damp canal, the soulless school buildings. The weather and locations will mirror the comedic deflation of the plot. **What I Intend to Aim For:** 1. **Compelling Character Dynamic:** Leo and Moose's friendship must feel real. Their dialogue should be funny, but also reveal genuine care and trust (even when they're annoyed with each other). 2. **Consistent Subversion:** Every time the story seems to be heading into genuine danger or high stakes, it will swerve into the absurd or mundane. 3. **A Satisfying Punchline:** The final reveal must be both anticlimactic and perfectly logical. The reader should feel the same mix of relief and embarrassment as the characters. 4. **Authentic Teen Voice:** The characters should sound and act like actual British teenagers, not adults writing "how do you do, fellow kids." **What I Intend to Avoid:** 1. **Actual Danger or Trauma:** No one is genuinely hurt, kidnapped, or in peril. The "darkness" is in the tone and the characters' overactive imaginations. 2. **Mean-Spirited Humour:** The comedy should not come from mocking the characters or their circumstances in a cruel way. We should laugh *with* Leo and Moose, not *at* them. 3.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# Human vs. LLM Approaches: A Deep Dive Critique ## How a Human Might Approach This Prompt A human writer, given the prompt *"Darkly Comic British YA Fiction: The text came just after midnight: 🩸🩸🩸"*, would likely start with **feelings and memories** rather than structure. They'd think: * "This reminds me of when my friend sent me a weird text at 3am and I couldn't sleep." * "What's the funniest way this could go wrong?" * "I want to write something that makes people laugh out loud on the bus." A human would probably: - **Write scenes out of order**—whatever's funniest or most vivid comes first, then they'd figure out how to connect them. - **Let the characters surprise them.** They'd start writing Leo and Moose, and suddenly Moose says something they didn't plan, and it changes the direction. - **Leave things unresolved.** A human might not care if every subplot ties up neatly. They'd prioritize what's funny or true over what's structurally complete. - **Include specific, weird details from their own life**—the actual name of a terrible local restaurant, the specific way their school's heating system clanked, the real texture of being 16 and bored in a drab town. - **Write badly first.** A human's first draft would be messy, uneven, sometimes too long in the wrong places, and contain at least one scene they love that doesn't quite fit but they can't bring themselves to cut. ## Common LLM Failings in Creative Writing **1. The Safety Blanket** LLMs default to "safe" choices. Every character is ultimately good-hearted. Every conflict resolves cleanly. Nobody says anything truly awkward, offensive, or real. In YA, this produces characters who sound like they've been through therapy—they articulate their feelings perfectly and validate each other constantly. Real teenagers are messy, sometimes selfish, and often don't know what they feel. **2. The Bow on Top** LLMs love resolution. Every thread gets tied. Every character gets a "moment." The ending must be *meaningful*. But dark comedy often lives in irresolution—in the joke that doesn't land, the apology that never comes, the question that remains unanswered. **3. Impressive Prose Over Readable Prose** LLMs tend to write "up"—toward complexity, toward literary flourishes, toward the kind of prose that would impress a writing tutor. But British YA dark comedy needs to be **down**—plain, punchy, and funny. Think Adrian Mole, not Ian McEwan. **4. The Thesaurus Problem** LLMs reach for unusual words to seem "well-written." But teens don't say "perfunctory" or "febrile." The vocabulary should be *accurate* to the character, not impressive to the reader. **5. Explaining the Joke** LLMs often undermine their own comedy by explaining why something is funny, or by adding a reflective line that "elevates" the moment. Dark comedy is often funnier when it's left hanging—when the reader has to sit with the discomfort. **6. The Moral Lesson** LLMs gravitate toward "and they learned something." But dark comedy doesn't require a lesson. Sometimes the takeaway is: "Well, that was pointless and embarrassing." **7. Flat Characterization Through Over-Description** LLMs describe characters by listing traits ("He was witty but insecure, with a tendency to overthink") rather than **showing** it through specific actions, dialogue, and behavior. A human writer would *show* Leo being witty by writing actual witty lines, not by telling us he's witty. **8. The Wikipedia Tone** LLMs can slip into a neutral, encyclopedic register that drains all personality from the narration. "Leo found the situation amusing" instead of a line that actually *is* amusing. **9. Simile Overkill** LLMs love similes. Every other sentence gets a comparison. "The rain fell like..." "He felt like..." "The silence was like..." A human writer knows that sometimes the funniest line is just a flat statement of fact. **10. No Real Stakes** LLMs struggle to create genuine tension because they're always hedging. "Leo felt a slight twinge of concern" instead of committing to a feeling. Or they oscillate between melodrama ("His heart hammered like a trapped bird") and deflation ("Actually, it was just a cat"), which becomes a predictable pattern rather than a genuine surprise. --- ## Deep Dive Critique of the Current Plan **Problem 1: The Plan is Too Neat** The current plan is *extremely* structured. Eight chapters, each with a clear beat. This is exactly how an LLM approaches writing—by creating a perfect scaffold before putting a single interesting sentence on the page. A human might have a loose sense of direction but would discover the story *in the writing*. The current plan risks producing a story that feels like it's checking boxes rather than unfolding naturally. *Human fix:* Write the scenes that excite you first. Let the structure emerge from the content, not the other way around. **Problem 2: The Characters Are "Types"** Leo is "the cynical one." Moose is "the anxious one." Leo's mum is "the quirky parent." These are character *descriptions*, not characters. A human writer would know that the most interesting character moments come from **contradiction**—the cynical kid who secretly loves his mum's terrible smoothies, the anxious kid who, when actually scared, becomes eerily calm and competent. *Human fix:* Give each character at least one trait that contradicts their "type." Let them do something in chapter 3 that you didn't plan, and follow it. **Problem 3: The Mystery Structure is Formulaic** The plan follows a classic detective structure: clue -> investigation -> suspect -> reveal. This is safe. A human dark comedy might have the investigation go in circles, or have the protagonists discover the truth in chapter 3 and spend the next five chapters unable to convince anyone, or have multiple "solves" that each turn out to be wrong. *Human fix:* Consider a structure where the mystery is solved early but nobody believes them, or where the comedy comes from them *knowing* the truth but being unable to do anything about it. **Problem 4: The Anticlimax is Predictable** The plan says: "The final reveal must be both anticlimactic and perfectly logical." But if every chapter is a deflation, the reader will see the punchline coming by chapter 4. The comedy in dark humor often comes from the **unexpected**—sometimes you need a moment of genuine sweetness in the middle of the cynicism, or a moment of real fear before the bathetic reveal. *Human fix:* Include at least one moment where something genuinely good or genuinely scary happens, so the anticlimax isn't just "another deflation." **Problem 5: The Setting is Generic "British Drab"** "The drab, rain-soaked town" is a cliché. A human writer from, say, Stoke-on-Trent would write about *Stoke-on-Trent*—the specific roundabouts, the specific smell of the canal, the specific way the local Co-op closes at 4pm. The current plan's setting is a composite "Britishness" that could be anywhere, which means it's actually nowhere. *Human fix:* Set it in a real town, or invent a town so specific it feels real. What's the terrible local takeaway? What's the one good thing about the town that everyone secretly loves? **Problem 6: The "Mock-Serious" Tone Could Become Monotonous** If every sentence is wryly undercutting itself, the prose will become exhausting. A human writer knows when to let a moment breathe—to write a passage that's just *sad*, or just *quiet*, before the next joke lands. *Human fix:* Plan for at least one chapter or extended scene that's genuinely tender or melancholic, with no jokes. Let the characters be vulnerable in a way that isn't immediately undercut. **Problem 7: The Plan Doesn't Account for Boring Bits** Real life—and real stories—have boring bits. The waiting. The scrolling through phones. The walking to places. A human writer often includes these moments because they're *true*, and sometimes the comedy or pathos lives in the mundane. The current plan is all "beats"—no texture. *Human fix:* Include scenes where nothing happens. Let the characters sit in a car, or wait in a corridor, and just... talk. Some of the best dialogue comes from "filler." **Problem 8: The Moral Compass is Too Clear** The plan says "no one is genuinely hurt." But real dark comedy often involves people being *slightly* hurt—not traumatised, but bruised. A friendship that's strained. A reputation that's damaged. An awkwardness that doesn't fully resolve. The current plan is too kind to its characters. *Human fix:* Let the story leave a mark. Maybe Leo and Moose's friendship is slightly different at the end—not broken, but changed. Maybe someone's feelings are genuinely hurt and the apology is awkward and incomplete. --- ## Brainstorming: Ideas to Make It More Human **1. Specificity Bombs** Instead of "the all-night diner," what if it's a specific, real-feeling place: "Dave's 24-Hour Café, which was never open 24 hours and was run by a woman named Sandra who called everyone 'love' and had a tattoo of a pineapple on her wrist." **2. The Unplanned Moment** Write a scene where Leo accidentally says something genuinely mean to Moose—not as a plot point, but just because he's frustrated and it slips out. Don't have him apologize immediately. Let it sit. **3. The Boring Chapter** What if one chapter is mostly just Leo and Moose walking to the canal, and the dialogue is about something completely unrelated to the mystery—like arguing about whether a particular YouTuber is funny or not. The mystery recedes. The friendship is the point. **4. The Genuinely Spooky Moment** In the middle of the comedy, have one moment that's actually unsettling. Maybe they find something at the pump house that's not funny—like a child's shoe, or a diary entry that's clearly written by someone in real distress. It changes the tone for a chapter before the bathos returns. **5. The Flawed Protagonist** What if Leo isn't just "cynical"—what if he's kind of a bad friend sometimes? What if his "investigation" is partly driven by ego, and Moose calls him out on it? **6. The Parent Who Isn't Quirky** What if one of the parents isn't a comic relief character? What if Leo's dad is just... tired. Quiet. Not funny. A presence that makes Leo feel guilty without doing anything wrong. **7. The Incomplete Apology** At the end, when they discover the truth, what if someone they suspected gets wind of it, and Leo has to apologize, but he's terrible at it, and the apology is awkward and incomplete and the other person just sort of stares at him and says "...okay" and walks away. No closure. Just cringe. **8. The Unsolved Thread** What if there's a minor subplot that never gets resolved? Like, who keeps leaving old VHS tapes in the school library? It's mentioned twice, never solved, and that's fine. Life is full of unsolved mysteries. **9. The Phone Itself** What if we make the phone a character? Leo's phone is cracked, the battery dies at the worst moments, autocorrect mangles his texts. Technology in real teen life is frustrating and specific, not a seamless plot device. **10. The Final Image** Instead of ending with them back in Leo's room "exhausted and mildly traumatised," what if the final image is something small and specific and a bit sad? Like Leo scrolling through his phone in bed, seeing the original text, and just... leaving it unread. No grand reflection. Just the blue light of the screen and the sound of rain. --- ## Summary of Key Humanization Priorities 1. **Embrace messiness**—in structure, character, and resolution. 2. **Be specific**—names, places, brands, textures. 3. **Let characters be contradictory**—and let them surprise you. 4. **Include boring bits**—they're often where the truth lives. 5. **Resist the bow**—not everything needs to be tied up. 6. **Allow tonal variation**—let it be genuinely sad or scary sometimes. 7. **Write for the reader, not the critic**—aim for funny and true, not impressive and complete. These are all critiques and ideas. No rewriting of the plan yet. But this analysis should inform the next stage of planning.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention **Core Concept:** Two sixteen-year-old boys in a forgettable Midlands town receive a cryptic, ominous text—three blood-drop emojis—just after midnight. What begins as a half-serious "investigation" spirals into an escalating series of awkward, embarrassing, and increasingly pointless misadventures. The story is a dark comedy about boredom, friendship, and the embarrassing gulf between how dramatic you think your life is and how dramatic it actually is. **What This Story Is Not:** It is not a thriller. It is not a mystery with a satisfying solution. It is not a story where characters learn and grow in a tidy arc. It is a story about two boys making idiots of themselves for eight chapters and coming out the other side slightly changed, but not in any way they could articulate. **Plot Direction:** The mystery of the blood-drop emojis is real, and the answer is genuinely anticlimactic—a wrong number from a year-nine kid trying to arrange a secret D&D session. But between the text and the answer, the boys will accuse the wrong people, damage a friendship, embarrass themselves publicly, and spend a genuinely uncomfortable night in a derelict building. The comedy is in the journey, not the destination. There will be one moment of genuine fear (chapter 5) and one moment of genuine hurt (chapter 7) that the comedy does not immediately undercut. These moments are what make the funny bits funny. If everything is ironic, nothing is. **Voice & Tone:** - **Narrative Voice:** Close third-person locked to Leo. His interiority is the engine of the comedy—sarcastic, movie-literate, and prone to narrating his own life as if it's a film he's directing. But the narration will sometimes falter. Sometimes Leo's inner voice goes quiet, and those moments will be written plainly, without wit, and they will matter more than the jokes. - **Dialogue:** Sharp, interrupted, realistic. People talk over each other. People say "like" and "you know" and "thingy." People trail off. The funniest lines will often be the ones said completely straight. - **Tone:** Mostly wry and deflating. But the story will allow itself to be genuinely tender in two or three small moments—without announcing them, without underlining them. A hand on a shoulder. A silence that means something. - **Humour Sources:** Failed plans. Social awkwardness. The absurdity of taking yourself seriously at sixteen. The specific texture of being bored in a specific place. Parents who mean well. Teachers who don't care. The rain. **Voice & Dialogue Specificity:** - Leo thinks in film metaphors ("This is the bit where the soundtrack swells") but he's not a snob—he watches YouTube as much as cinema. His references will be mixed and slightly wrong, which is funnier than being precise. - Moose doesn't banter so much as **state things flatly**. His humour is deadpan. "We're going to die in a pump house" is funnier said without inflection. - Their dialogue will include the boring, connective tissue of real conversation—filler, half-questions, comfortable silences—rather than being wall-to-wall punchlines. **Setting:** Wefton. A fictional small town in the West Midlands that is not quite bad enough to be interesting. It has a canal, a retail park, a cinema with three screens, and a high street that's half charity shops. The specific details of Wefton will emerge through incidental mentions rather than description dumps. The town should feel like a character you recognize but can't quite name. **Character Work:** - **Leo Finch:** Not just "the cynical one." He uses sarcasm as armour. He's funny, but his comedy is partly a defence against the fact that he doesn't know how to be sincere without feeling stupid. He loves his mum but is embarrassed by her. He is a bad friend sometimes—not cruelly, but carelessly. He doesn't always notice when Moose is genuinely upset because he's too busy performing. - **Marcus "Moose" Okoro:** Not just "the anxious one." He's anxious, yes, but he's also stubborn, quietly competitive, and has a moral compass that occasionally makes him annoying. He keeps a list of suspects in his phone's notes app with colour-coded confidence ratings. He is more observant than Leo, and occasionally says something devastatingly perceptive that Leo ignores. He is the better friend of the two, and both of them know it, and neither of them says it. - **Leo's Mum, Karen:** A real person, not a quirky prop. She runs a small Etsy shop selling candles with names like "Serenity" and "Forest Bathing." She is genuinely kind, slightly lonely, and has no idea how to parent a sixteen-year-old boy. She is funny because she's real, not because she's "wacky." - **Sandra, who runs Dave's 24-Hour Café:** She calls everyone "love." She has seen everything. She doesn't care about the boys' mystery but will sell them chips at 1am without asking questions. **Stylistic Choices:** - **Chapter length discipline:** Each chapter is roughly 1000 words. This means every scene must earn its place. No scenic descriptions that don't serve character or comedy. No dialogue exchanges that go in circles. Tight, purposeful, but with room for the small human moments that make it feel alive. - **The 3am Chapter:** Chapter 5, the night at the pump house, will be written in a slightly different register—slower, more sensory, genuinely creepy for a stretch. This is deliberate. The tonal shift makes the return to comedy in chapter 6 land harder. - **Imperfect structure:** The "investigation" doesn't follow a clean A→B→C logic. The boys go in circles. They revisit old suspects. They argue about things that have nothing to do with the mystery. This is intentional. Real investigations by sixteen-year-olds are messy. - **No thesis statement.** The story does not end with a reflection on what they learned. It ends with a small, specific image and the quiet understanding that some things don't mean anything, and that's fine. **What I Intend to Aim For:** 1. **Specificity over generality.** Every place, every character detail, every throwaway reference should feel observed, not invented. 2. **Earned tonal shifts.** The genuinely scary moment (chapter 5) and the genuinely hurt moment (chapter 7) must feel like they belong, not like tonal whiplash. 3. **An anticlimax that feels like a relief.** The final reveal should make the reader laugh and sigh simultaneously. Not "oh, clever" but "oh, for God's sake." 4. **Characters who feel like people you know.** Leo and Moose should feel like actual sixteen-year-old boys, not adult ideas of sixteen-year-old boys. 5. **An ending that doesn't explain itself.** The final image should resonate without being explained. Trust the reader. **What I Intend to Avoid:** 1. **The neat bow.** Not every thread resolves. Not every relationship is repaired fully. The apology in chapter 7 is partial and awkward. 2. **The moral.** There is no lesson. There is only the experience. 3. **Impressive prose.** The writing should be invisible when it's working. Plain, precise, occasionally beautiful by accident. 4. **Explaining the joke.** If a line needs a follow-up sentence to explain why it's funny, cut the follow-up. 5. **Symmetrical structure.** Not every chapter mirrors another. Some chapters are longer on dialogue, some on action, some on interiority. Variety within constraint. --- # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1: Midnight (≈1000 words)** Leo is in bed, scrolling, half-asleep. The phone buzzes. Three blood drops. He stares at it. Screenshots it. Sends it to Moose with "???" Moose replies instantly ("Why are you awake" then "What the hell" then "Is this from Dan"). They text back and forth—a real texting exchange, with typos and delays and one message that just says "haha" because Moose doesn't know what else to say. They decide it's Dan, their friend, being an edgelord. They make a vague plan to "get him back" tomorrow. Leo lies in the dark, staring at the ceiling. The chapter ends not on a joke but on the specific feeling of receiving something strange at midnight and not being able to shake it. One paragraph. Plain. No comedy. *Purpose:* Establish the mystery. Establish the friendship (through texting rhythm). Establish Leo's voice. End on unease rather than punchline. **Chapter 2: The Revenge (≈1000 words)** Next morning at school. Leo and Moose confront Dan, who has no idea what they're talking about. Dan is genuinely confused, slightly hurt they'd think he'd send something like that. The three of them check Dan's phone—no sent message. Awkward silence. Leo, feeling stupid, doubles down: "Well, someone sent it." They hatch a half-baked plan to send something scary back to the mystery number—a photo of a clown, or something. They do it at lunch, giggling, feeling clever. The chapter ends with the reply: a single question mark. Then, five minutes later: a photo of the outside of their school. Taken that day. They recognise the angle—it was taken from the car park, near where they were standing at lunch. *Purpose:* Establish Dan as a real character (not just a plot device). The failed revenge introduces genuine unease. The photo of the school escalates the stakes without any actual danger. End on a hook that's creepy but could still be mundane. **Chapter 3: The List (≈1000 words)** Freaked out by the photo, they make a suspect list. This chapter is mostly dialogue—the two of them in Leo's bedroom after school, throwing out names, arguing, getting distracted. The suspect list is ridiculous. It includes: the new kid (Ellis), who wears a black coat and "looks like he'd send blood emojis"; Mrs. Barker, their history teacher, who once confiscated Moose's phone and "knows things"; a girl named Priya who Leo had a crush on last year and who once said his haircut was "interesting" (Leo insists this could be a coded threat). Moose's methodical notes app list vs. Leo's chaotic energy. Karen pops in with smoothies and asks if they're "collaborating on homework" and they say yes and she looks pleased and leaves. The chapter ends with Moose finding the number on a reverse-search website: it's a burner phone, registered to no one. The mystery deepens. Leo feels a thrill. Moose feels sick. *Purpose:* Character work. Comedy through the absurdity of the suspect list. The mum interaction humanises Leo. The burner phone detail adds a layer of genuine intrigue while remaining plausible (burner phones are cheap and available). Establish the Leo/Moose dynamic—Leo performs, Moose plans. **Chapter 4: Suspect Zero (≈1000 words)** They focus on Ellis, the new kid. He wears black, keeps to himself, and once drew a "weird symbol" in the margin of his maths book (it was a doodle of a Rubik's cube, but Leo has decided it's a sigil). They follow him after school to the retail park, where he enters the Card Factory. They wait outside, narrating their surveillance in hushed, dramatic voices. Ellis emerges with a birthday card. He's buying a birthday card. They tail him to the bus stop, where he sits alone, and suddenly Leo feels a pang of something—he recognises the specific loneliness of a kid sitting alone at a bus stop with a birthday card. He doesn't say anything. They lose him at the next stop. That night, Leo texts Moose: "Maybe it's not Ellis." Moose replies: "Obviously." The chapter ends with Leo alone, feeling oddly ashamed, though he couldn't say why. *Purpose:* Introduce Ellis as a real person, not a suspect. The surveillance is funny (Card Factory), but the moment at the bus stop introduces the first crack in Leo's detective fantasy. He's starting to see people as people, not characters in his story. The shame is new and unwelcome. **Chapter 5: The Pump House (≈1000 words)** A new text arrives: a location pin for the Victorian pump house by the canal. They go at night. This chapter is the tonal shift. The writing slows down. The pump house is genuinely unsettling—broken windows, the smell of damp and rust, the sound of water. They explore with phone torches. For about 400 words, it's actually scary—not thriller-scary, but the specific, childlike fear of being somewhere you're not supposed to be in the dark. Then Moose finds something: a sleeping bag, a tin of beans, a child's drawing of a house. Someone has been sleeping here. The fear curdles into something else—discomfort, pity. They don't know what to do. They leave quickly. Walking home, neither of them talks. The chapter ends with Moose saying, quietly: "We should tell someone." Leo says: "Yeah." They don't. *Purpose:* The genuine scare. The discovery of the sleeping bag changes the register—from "fun mystery" to "someone might actually be in trouble." They don't resolve it. They don't call the police. They're sixteen and scared and they go home. This is the moment the story stops being a game. **Chapter 6: The Dracula (≈1000
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Leo Finch **Background & Personality:** Leo is sixteen, lives with his mum Karen in a 1990s semi-detached in Wefton. His dad, Graham, left when Leo was twelve—not dramatically, not with slamming doors, but with the slow, quiet departure of a man who'd rather be somewhere else. Graham now lives in Telford with a woman who breeds Labradoodles. He texts Leo every other Sunday with messages like "How's school? 😊" and Leo replies "fine" and that's the whole relationship. This hasn't made Leo tragic. It's made him watchful. He notices when people are about to leave a room before they know it themselves. Leo is funny. Genuinely, naturally funny—the kind of funny that makes other people's days slightly better. But he's also aware that being funny is easier than being honest, and he's been using it as a deflection for so long that he's not entirely sure what's underneath anymore. He narrates his own life constantly, casting himself as the protagonist in a film he's directing in his head. When something bad happens, his first instinct is "this will make a good story," which is useful for comedy and terrible for empathy. He's not a bad kid. He does his homework (mostly), he's kind to his mum (mostly), he doesn't bully anyone. But he's careless with people sometimes—not maliciously, just with the casual selfishness of a teenager who hasn't yet learned that other people's feelings are as real and complicated as his own. He'll make a joke at Moose's expense and not notice the flicker of hurt, because he's too busy enjoying the laugh. **Core Details:** - Obsessed with films but his taste is chaotic—he loves both *Hot Fuzz* and genuinely terrible horror films from the 2000s. He quotes them constantly, slightly wrong. - Has a half-finished screenplay on his laptop called "DEAD FREQUENCY" about a radio DJ who receives coded messages from the future. It's not good, but he doesn't know that yet. - Eats cereal for dinner at least three nights a week when Karen's working late at the candle business. His favourite is Golden Grahams, which he eats in a specific bowl—a blue one with a chip on the rim—and he would not be able to explain why this matters to him. - His bedroom is a mess, but a specific mess: the desk is chaos, but his bookshelf is meticulously organized by genre. The contradiction is unconscious. - Once cried at the end of *Marley & Me* and told Moose it was allergies. Moose didn't believe him but didn't push it. **Trivial Details:** - Bites his nails to the quick. His right thumbnail is permanently ragged. - Has a Spotify playlist called "soundtrack for walking places" that is mostly Arctic Monkeys and Radiohead, which he'd be embarrassed if anyone found because it's a bit pretentious. - Can't swim. Never learned. This has never come up but it's there. - Owns exactly one hoodie (grey, Nike, getting thin at the elbows) that he wears approximately 80% of the time. Karen keeps buying him new ones. He keeps not wearing them. **Physicality:** Tallish but doesn't carry it well—slouches, hands in pockets, shoulders curved inward as if trying to take up less space. Brown hair that's too long because he keeps forgetting to book a haircut. Face that's pleasant but not remarkable—people describe him as "Dan's friend" or "the tall one" before they remember his name. He moves through the world like someone who's slightly embarrassed to be occupying space. When he's performing (telling a story, making a joke), he comes alive—hands moving, eyes bright. When he's not performing, he sort of recedes. His expressions are exaggerated when he's being funny—eyebrows up, mouth open, the whole face engaged. When he's being genuine, his face goes still and slightly blank, as if sincerity requires him to turn off the performance software. **Motive & Relationships:** Leo's primary motive at the start is **boredom**. Wefton is boring. School is boring. The blood-drop text is the most interesting thing that's happened to him in months, and he latches onto it not because he's brave or curious but because the alternative is scrolling TikTok until his eyes hurt. As the story progresses, his motive shifts to **ego**—he's the one who started this, he's the one who's narrating it, and he doesn't want to be the protagonist of a story that turns out to be nothing. His relationship with **Moose** is the most important in his life, and he doesn't fully appreciate it. Moose is his person—the one who gets his jokes, who fills in the silences, who doesn't ask too many questions. Leo takes this for granted in the way that people take gravity for granted: it's just there. He will, in chapter 7, say something careless that genuinely hurts Moose, and the realization that he's capable of hurting his best friend will be one of the story's quiet, unspoken turning points. With **Karen**, his mum, it's love wrapped in embarrassment. He loves her. He's mortified by her. He would fight someone for her but would also rather die than be seen hugging her in public. She represents home—safe, known, slightly suffocating. With **Dan**, it's simpler. Dan is fun. Dan is easy. Dan is the friend you go to the cinema with, not the friend you text at midnight. Dan is background friendship—real but shallow. **Flavour Dialogue / Internal Monologue:** *Internal monologue, watching Ellis at the bus stop:* > He was just sitting there. Just a kid on a bench with a birthday card, waiting for a bus in the rain. And something about it—the way his shoulders were hunched, the way he was holding the card against his chest to keep it dry—made Leo think of himself at twelve, sitting on the kerb outside his dad's new place in Telford, waiting for a lift that was twenty minutes late. He didn't say anything. What would he even say? "Hey, sorry we followed you to a Card Factory, you looked less murderer-ish than we thought"? *Dialogue, arguing with Moose about suspects:* > "Right, but think about it logically," Moose said, in the voice he used when he was about to say something Leo wouldn't like. > "I don't want to think about it logically. I want to think about it *cinematically*." > "That's not a word." > "It is. It's the word for what I'm doing." > "What you're doing is accusing Mrs. Barker of sending us death threats because she confiscated my phone once." > "She confiscated it with *malice*, Moose." > "She confiscated it because you were playing Subway Surfers in double maths." --- # Marcus "Moose" Okoro **Background & Personality:** Moose is sixteen, lives with both parents and his younger sister Tia (fourteen, insufferable, currently in a phase where she refers to everything as "iconic" or "a red flag") in a slightly nicer semi-detached two streets over from Leo's. His mum is a district nurse; his dad works at the Jaguar Land Rover plant in Solihull and comes home smelling of machine oil and quiet exhaustion. The Okoro household is warm, busy, and organized in a way that Leo's isn't—there's a chore rota on the fridge, family dinners on Sundays, and his dad makes jollof rice on Saturdays that fills the whole house with the smell of tomato and scotch bonnet. Moose earned his nickname in Year 7 when he got stuck in a doorframe during a fire drill—not because he was large, but because he panicked and tried to go through sideways. The name stuck because it was funny, and Moose accepted it with the pragmatism that defines him. His real name is Marcus, and only his mum and his form tutor use it. He is anxious. This is not a cute, quirky anxiety—it's a real, physiological thing that manifests as a tightness in his chest before social situations, a tendency to catastrophize, and a near-compulsive need to plan for worst-case scenarios. He manages it. He breathes. He makes lists. He has a CBT worksheet on his phone that he's supposed to fill in daily and mostly doesn't. But it's there, and it shapes everything—his caution, his need for control, his occasional rigidity. But here's the thing about Moose that Leo doesn't always see: he's also quietly fierce. He has opinions and he holds them. He once argued with a supply teacher for twenty minutes about the accuracy of a historical timeline, calmly and with evidence, until the teacher gave up. He doesn't back down when he thinks he's right, which makes him both admirable and occasionally infuriating. He is the moral compass of the pair, and like all moral compasses, he's not always pointing where you want him to. **Core Details:** - Keeps a suspect list in his phone's notes app with colour-coded confidence ratings (red = high, amber = medium, green = "probably not but can't rule out"). The system is meticulous and slightly unhinged. - Brilliant at maths. Not in a prodigy way, but in a "gets A* and makes it look effortless" way. He doesn't flaunt it, but there's a quiet satisfaction in it. - His bedroom is tidy. Not fanatically so, but in the way that suggests he finds mess genuinely stressful. His desk has a desk tidy. His books are upright. There is a plant—a small cactus named Gerald—that he waters every Sunday. - Plays five-a-side football on Saturday mornings, not because he loves it but because his dad wants him to and it gets him out of the house. He's not bad at it. He's not good either. He's reliable, which is the football equivalent of being "nice." - Has a complicated relationship with his own competence. He knows he's clever, and he knows that being clever and anxious is an exhausting combination, and he sometimes resents that Leo gets to be funny and careless while he has to be careful and responsible. **Trivial Details:** - Eats toast with an irrational amount of butter. His mum once weighed the butter he put on a single slice: 28 grams. He was unapologetic. - Has never seen *Hot Fuzz*, which Leo considers a personal failing. - His phone case is plain black. He's considered getting a novelty one but can't decide, so he's had the plain black one for two years. - Can do a perfect impression of their headteacher, Mr. Rudge, that he only does when he's sure no teachers are within earshot. It's note-perfect and slightly terrifying. - Once helped Leo bury a dead bird in Leo's garden and said a few words over it ("It was probably a pigeon. It doesn't care. But fine."), which Leo has never forgotten, though neither of them has ever spoken about it again. **Physicality:** Shorter than Leo by a good four inches, and stocky—not fat, but solid, with the build of someone who eats well and moves regularly. He has a round face that makes him look younger than he is, close-cropped hair that his mum cuts, and dark brown skin that he's comfortable in. He carries himself with a stillness that can read as either calm or braced for impact—there's a tension in his posture, a readiness, as if his body is always half-prepared for something to go wrong. When he's relaxed—and he's relaxed around Leo more than anyone—his face opens up. He smiles easily, and it transforms him, makes him look like the kid he actually is rather than the responsible adult he sometimes pretends to be. When he's anxious, he goes very still and very quiet, and his eyes fix on a middle distance, and you can almost see him running scenarios behind them. He gestures minimally when he talks. One hand, mostly. A finger pointing, a palm upturned, a dismissive wave. His body language says "I'm here, I'm present, I'm not going to make a scene." **Motive & Relationships:** Moose's primary motive is **loyalty**. He doesn't care about the mystery—he thinks it's probably nothing, and he'd rather be doing literally anything else. But Leo cares, and Leo is his best friend, and that's enough. He follows Leo into the investigation not because he's curious but because he can't imagine not following. This loyalty is both his greatest strength and his biggest vulnerability, because it means he'll go along with things he knows are stupid, and he'll absorb damage that isn't his to absorb. His secondary motive is **control**. The suspect list, the plans, the contingencies—these aren't just anxiety management. They're Moose's way of imposing order on a world that feels chaotic. The mystery threatens this order, and as it escalates, so does his need to manage it, which puts him in tension with Leo's chaotic approach. With **Leo**, it's complicated. Moose loves Leo—platonically, fiercely, with the uncomplicated intensity of a sixteen-year-old boy who doesn't have the language for what this friendship means. But he's also aware that the friendship is slightly unequal. Leo talks, Moose listens. Leo performs, Moose is the audience. Moose doesn't mind, mostly, because he prefers listening, and because when Leo is funny, he's genuinely, brilliantly funny, and being around that energy is its own kind of joy. But sometimes—rarely, in quiet moments—Moose wonders what it would be like to have a friend who asked him how he was and waited for the answer. With **his family**, it's love and mild suffocation. His parents are good people who want the best for him, and their expectations (good grades, good behaviour, good future) feel like a warm blanket that's occasionally too heavy. His sister Tia drives him up the wall, but he'd also fight anyone who hurt her. **Flavour Dialogue / Internal Monologue:** *Internal monologue, in the pump house:* > The torch beam wobbled over concrete and rust and something that might have been moss or might have been mould, and Moose thought: *I am in a derelict building at half past eleven at night with my best friend, who thinks this is a film, and I am going to die here, and my mum will have to identify my body, and Tia will cry for exactly one day before she gets my room.* *Dialogue, after the failed confrontation with Dan:* > "So to be clear," Moose said, "we accused our friend of sending us a threatening text message, he didn't do it, and now he thinks we're paranoid weirdos." > "He doesn't think we're paranoid weirdos." > "He said, and I quote, 'You lot are actually mental.'" > "That's just Dan being Dan." > "That's Dan being *accurate*." --- # Karen Finch **Background & Personality:** Karen is forty-two, Leo's mum, and she is doing her best. This is not a euphemism for "she's failing"—she's genuinely doing well, all things considered. She works part-time at the Wefton library (Tuesday, Thursday, Saturday mornings) and runs her Etsy shop, "Grove & Glow," from the kitchen table, making soy candles with names like "Forest Bathing," "Sunday Morning," and the bestseller, "F*cking Monday" (the asterisk is load-bearing; Etsy's content policy is a nightmare). The business does okay. Not great. Okay. She and Graham separated four years ago. She doesn't hate him—hate would require energy she doesn't have—but there's a low, steady resentment that surfaces in small ways. She never speaks badly of him in front of Leo, which is both admirable and slightly painful, because Leo sometimes wishes she'd just be angry rather than magnanimous. She started the candle business after the divorce, partly for money, partly for something to do with her hands, partly because she needed to prove to herself that she could build something. The candles are good. The labels are hand-written. She's proud of them in a quiet, fierce way that she doesn't often show. Karen is not a "cool mum." She tries sometimes—she's attempted to learn TikTok dances, she once suggested they watch a film together and picked *The Kissing Booth*—but she's fundamentally a woman of her generation, slightly out of step with her son's world, and she knows it. She doesn't push. She's present, she's warm, she makes sure there's food in the fridge, and she gives Leo space when he needs it, which is most of the time. This is good parenting. It's also a little lonely. **Core Details:** - Her candle workshop is the kitchen table, covered in newspaper and wax drips. The kitchen smells permanently of lavender and cedar, which Leo pretends to hate but actually finds comforting. - She listens to podcasts while making candles—true crime, mostly, which is ironic given the plot of this story. She once tried to explain the Dyatlov Pass incident to Leo over breakfast and he nearly choked on his toast. - She has a friend named Deb who she sees on Friday nights for wine and *Strictly*. Deb is her lifeline. Without Deb, Karen would talk to no one over the age of sixteen about anything real. - She worries about Leo. Not in a hovering way, but in a 3am-staring-at-the-ceiling way. She knows he misses his dad. She knows he uses humour to avoid things. She doesn't know how to fix it, and the not-knowing keeps her up some nights. - She calls Leo "love" and "chick" and once, memorably, "my little disaster," which Leo pretended to hate and secretly liked. **Trivial Details:** - Has a favourite mug (cream, chipped handle, says "But First, Coffee" which she finds endlessly funny for reasons she can't explain). - Her car is a 2014 Fiat 500 in mint green that she's had since new and will drive until it dies. - She once tried to learn guitar during the divorce and gave up after three lessons. The guitar lives in the corner of the living room, untouched, a monument to good intentions. - She is an extremely average cook but makes a very good spag bol, which is the one meal she knows Leo will always eat. - She has never once successfully set the house alarm, despite having it for five years. She just forgets. **Physicality:** Medium height, medium build, brown hair that she ties up with a clip when she's working and lets down when she's not. Her face is kind—that's the first and last thing anyone would say about her. She smiles easily, with her whole face, and when she's worried, it shows in her eyes before anywhere else. She dresses practically—jeans, jumpers, the occasional scarf—and moves through the kitchen with the automatic efficiency of someone who knows where everything is. She has a habit of touching things as she passes them—a hand on the doorframe, fingertips along the counter—as if she's confirming the world is still solid. When she's anxious, she cleans. When she's very anxious, she makes candles at 2am, and the house fills with the smell of wax and essential oils, and Leo wakes to the smell and feels, without knowing why, that something is wrong. **Motive & Relationships:** Karen's motive is **protection**. She wants Leo to be safe and happy, and she doesn't know how to guarantee either, so she settles for being present and hoping for the best. She is not a helicopter parent—she gives Leo freedom, maybe too much, because she remembers being sixteen and hating being watched. But there's a tension in this: she wants to know what's happening in his life, and he won't tell her, and she respects the boundary, and it hurts. With **Leo**, it's a love that is obvious and unspoken and slightly awkward. They don't have deep conversations. They have small ones—"How was school?" "Fine." "Want some toast?" "Yeah."—that are the actual texture of their relationship. Karen would like more. Leo doesn't know how to give more. They're both doing their best, and their best is a quiet, functional love that doesn't look like much from the outside but holds the whole house together. With **Graham**, it's complicated. She doesn't talk about him. When Leo mentions his dad, she responds with careful neutrality ("Oh, that's nice" or "Right"), and the careful neutrality is louder than anger would be. She's moved on—she genuinely has—but there's a scar, and it aches sometimes in the weather. **Flavour Dialogue / Internal Monologue (rendered through Leo's POV):** *Karen, popping her head into Leo's room:* > "You two okay? Want some snacks? I've got those little breadsticks you like." > "We're fine, Mum." > "Okay, love. Just shout if you need anything." > She hovered for a second, like she wanted to say something else, and then didn't, and closed the door softly, and Leo felt the familiar mix of gratitude and guilt that she always left him with—that she was so careful with him, and he gave her so little back. *Karen, on the phone to Deb, overheard by Leo:* > "He's fine, I think. He's in his room a lot. I know, I know. He's sixteen. But sometimes I just wish he'd—I don't know. Talk to me about something that isn't school or toast." --- # Dan Kowalski **Background & Personality:** Dan is sixteen, Polish-British, third-generation, and his grandparents still make pierogi for Christmas that are better than anything you can buy. He's in Leo and Moose's friend group but occupies a different orbit—easier, lighter, less intense. He's the friend you see in groups, the one who's always up for the cinema or the arcade or a kickabout, but who you don't text at midnight because the midnight friendship is reserved for Leo and Moose. Dan is straightforward. This is both his strength and his limitation. He doesn't overthink, doesn't scheme, doesn't narrate his own life. He just... lives. He plays video games, he supports Wolves (by family obligation, not passion), he does okay at school without trying too hard, and he's happy in a way that Leo finds both admirable and faintly irritating. He doesn't have a "thing"—no obsession, no defence mechanism, no complicated inner life that he's armouring against. He's just Dan. And that's fine. It's just not very interesting, which is why he's not the protagonist. He's not stupid—far from it—but his intelligence is practical rather than cerebral. He's good with his hands (his dad's a joiner, and Dan can fix things), good at reading rooms, good at knowing when to leave a conversation that's getting too heavy. He's socially fluent in a way that Leo and Moose aren't, and he navigates the school ecosystem with an ease that looks effortless. **Core Details:** - Supports Wolves. His bedroom wall has a signed photo of Raúl Jiménez that his dad got him for his fourteenth birthday. - His dad's workshop is in their garage, and Dan sometimes helps out on weekends—sanding, measuring, holding things steady. He likes the work. He likes the smell of sawdust. He'd never admit this because it sounds like something your dad would say. - He's a good singer but doesn't sing. This is a buried detail—something that surfaces once, in a specific moment, and then retreats. He once hummed along to a song in the car and Leo said "that's actually good" and Dan said "shut up" and it never came up again. - He doesn't have Leo's wit or Moose's intensity, but he has something neither of them has: he's easy to be around. He doesn't demand anything. He's social oxygen. **Trivial Details:** - His phone wallpaper is his dog, a Jack Russell named Wojtek (after the bear). - He eats lunch the same way every day: sandwich, apple, chocolate bar, in that order. - He has a scar on his left knee from a bike crash in Year 8 that he's secretly proud of. - He once built a surprisingly good shelf in D&T that Mr. Hollis said was "excellent work," and Dan carried that compliment around for months. - He uses "sound" as a universal affirmative. Everything is either "sound" or "not sound." **Physicality:** Average height, lean, moves with an easy, athletic grace—like someone who's comfortable in his body. Sandy brown hair, blue eyes, a face that's open and slightly freckled. He smiles with his whole face, and his smile is the kind that makes other people smile back. He doesn't slouch like Leo or hold tension like Moose; he just... is. He occupies space comfortably. He has a habit of thumbs-upping everything.
