Not ironically, not performatively—just a genuine, uncomplicated thumbs-up that communicates "I acknowledge and accept this information." It's his default response to almost everything. **Motive & Relationships:** Dan's motive in the story is **none**, initially. He's a suspect, then he's not, and then he recedes into the background. But his role is important: he's the mirror. When Leo and Moose accuse him, Dan's genuine confusion and slight hurt show them—and the reader—what it looks like when your friends treat you like a suspect. It's the first crack in Leo's "this is a film" fantasy, even if he doesn't acknowledge it. With **Leo and Moose**, Dan is the easy friend. He likes them. He doesn't fully understand them, but he likes them. He finds Leo funny, finds Moose intense, and doesn't know that there's a depth to their friendship that excludes him. He's not jealous of it—he doesn't have the introspection for jealousy—but he's aware, in a vague way, that they have something he's not part of. **Flavour Dialogue:** *After being accused of sending the text:* > "Wait, you actually think I sent that?" Dan looked between them, genuinely baffled. "Why would I send you blood emojis? What would I even—what would be the point?" > "For a laugh," Leo said, but it sounded weak even to him. > "A laugh," Dan repeated. He looked at Moose. "Is he serious?" > "He's... working through some things," Moose said. > "I'm not working through things. I'm investigating." > "You're investigating *me*." > "Well, you're a suspect." > "I'm your mate!" > "Those aren't mutually exclusive," Leo said, and then immediately wished he hadn't, because Dan's face did something complicated and then went flat, and the flatness was worse than the bafflement. --- # Ellis Park **Background & Personality:** Ellis is sixteen, moved to Wefton from Birmingham three months ago after his parents' separation. He lives with his mum in a flat above the Co-op on the high street, and the flat is small and smells of the Co-op's bakery section, which sounds nice but isn't because it's the same smell, all the time, permanent, inescapable. He doesn't talk about Birmingham. He doesn't talk about his dad. He doesn't talk much at all. Ellis is not a goth, not an emo, not whatever Leo has decided he is. He wears a black coat because it was cheap and warm and his mum bought it for him from the charity shop. He keeps to himself because he's new and making friends is hard when you're the kid who arrived mid-term, and because he's naturally quiet, and because the quiet is easier than the alternative. He's not mysterious. He's tired. He's clever—top set everything—but doesn't flaunt it. His intelligence is the quiet kind, the kind that observes and absorbs and occasionally produces something unexpectedly brilliant in class that surprises everyone, including himself. He draws in the margins of his books—not "weird symbols" but doodles, Rubik's cubes and faces and small, intricate patterns that are actually rather good. He'd be embarrassed if anyone saw them. He is, in short, a normal kid going through a hard time, and the fact that Leo and Moose have cast him as a suspect says more about them than it does about him. **Core Details:** - His mum works nights at the Royal Shrewsbury Hospital, so he's alone in the flat most evenings. He eats dinner alone (usually toast or noodles), watches YouTube, does his homework, goes to bed. The flat is quiet in a way that is both peaceful and oppressive. - He has a Rubik's cube that he solves methodically, not quickly but with a meditative calm. It's his fidget object, his comfort thing. He can solve it in about ninety seconds, which isn't competition-level but is impressive enough. - He's good at art but doesn't take it for GCSE because his mum said it wasn't "useful," and he didn't argue because arguing with his mum is exhausting and she's already doing enough. - He once smiled in class—a real smile, not the polite one—and a girl named Aisha said "oh my god, you actually have a nice smile" and he went bright red and hasn't smiled in class since. **Trivial Details:** - His phone is a Samsung with a cracked screen that he's never got around to fixing. - He eats lunch alone, always in the same spot—a bench near the maths block that's out of the wind. - He owns three t-shirts, all dark colours, on rotation. - He once lent a pen to Moose in maths and Moose said "cheers" and Ellis said "no worries" and it was the most words they'd exchanged in three months. - He has never been to Dave's 24-Hour Café. **Physicality:** Slight, pale, dark hair that falls over his forehead in a way that looks effortless but is probably just unkempt. He has a face that will be handsome in a few years but currently looks unfinished—sharp features, large eyes, a mouth that defaults to neutral rather than smiling. He carries himself with a compactness, as if he's trying to minimize his presence, to be unnoticeable. He walks quickly, head down, earbuds in, even when nothing is playing—the earbuds are a wall. When he's focused on something (solving the cube, drawing), his body relaxes. His shoulders drop, his breathing slows, and he becomes, for a moment, the person he is when no one is watching. These moments are private, and the story respects them. **Motive & Relationships:** Ellis has **no motive** in the mystery. He is, genuinely, just a kid. His role in the story is to be the first moment where Leo's narrative—his "film"—collides with reality. When Leo sees Ellis at the bus stop with the birthday card, it's the first time he sees a suspect as a person, and the dissonance is uncomfortable and important. With **Leo and Moose**, Ellis doesn't have a relationship. He barely registers them. He has his own things—his mum, his Rubik's cube, his quiet flat above the Co-op—and the mystery that consumes Leo and Moose is invisible to him. This is important. He doesn't know he's been suspected. He doesn't know he's been followed. He's just living his life, and the fact that two boys from school have cast him as a villain in their private drama is both darkly comic and quietly sad. **Flavour Dialogue / Internal Monologue (from Ellis's perspective, not used in the story, but for character grounding):** *Ellis, thinking about Wefton:* > The canal was all right. He walked along it sometimes after school, when the light was low and the water went a colour that wasn't brown or green but something in between, and the ducks didn't care about him, which was nice. In Birmingham there were people everywhere, all the time, noise and movement and the constant hum of being known. Here it was quiet. Different kind of quiet. Not bad. Just different. He was still figuring out if he liked it. *Ellis, on being the new kid:* > He wasn't lonely. That's what he told himself. He was just... between things. Between friends, between homes, between the person he was in Birmingham and the person he was becoming here. It wasn't sad. It was just maths. An equation he hadn't solved yet. He'd get there. He always did. --- # Sandra (Dave's 24-Hour Café) **Background & Personality:** Sandra is fifty-something, runs the café that is named after a man named Dave who left in 2011 and she hasn't bothered to change the sign. The café is not open 24 hours. It's open from 6am to 2am, which is close enough, and Sandra considers the name aspirational rather than factual. She's been there for thirteen years, and in that time she's served chips to drunks, students, night-shift workers, insomniacs, and the occasional police officer, and she has never once asked anyone why they're eating chips at 1am because it's none of her business. Sandra is the kind of woman who has seen everything and is surprised by nothing. She's not warm in the conventional sense—she doesn't fuss, doesn't enquire, doesn't pry—but she's reliable. She'll serve you chips at 1am without asking questions, and that's its own kind of kindness. She has a tattoo of a pineapple on her wrist ("It was a dare. I was forty. Don't judge me.") and she calls everyone "love" regardless of age, gender, or context. She's a functional character in the story—she's there when the boys need chips and shelter—but she also represents a kind of adult wisdom that isn't teachy or preachy. She's the adult who doesn't try to be your friend, who doesn't try to "reach" you, who just exists in her own orbit and occasionally intersects with yours in ways that are useful and slightly mysterious. **Core Details:

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 1: Midnight Leo was in that place between scrolling and sleeping, where your thumb keeps moving but your brain has already clocked out, and the videos blur into one long stream of nothing—someone rating Greggs items, a cat falling off a shelf, a man explaining why the Roman Empire was actually just a vibes-based civilisation. His phone was warm in his hand. His room was dark except for the blue light on his face and the thin stripe of orange under his door where the hall light was still on because his mum never remembered to turn it off. The phone buzzed. Not a notification buzz—a proper buzz, the kind that vibrated through his hand and into his wrist and made his teeth feel weird. A text. Just after midnight, which was odd because the only people who texted Leo after midnight were spam bots and his dad, and his dad's texts usually came at a reasonable hour with a smiley face and a question about school that Leo would answer with "fine" three days later. He looked. Three red drops. That was it. No words, no context, no punctuation. Just: 🩸🩸🩸 Leo stared at it. He stared at it for what felt like a long time but was probably eight seconds. Then he screenshot it, because that's what you do when something weird happens—you preserve it, you make it evidence, you treat your life like it matters enough to document. He sent the screenshot to Moose. "???" Moose replied instantly, which meant he was also awake, which meant he was also scrolling, which meant neither of them would mention this tomorrow because admitting you were on your phone at midnight was social death. Moose: > why are you awake Leo: > why are YOU awake > look at this Moose: > what the hell > who sent that Leo: > no idea. just came through. Moose: > is it dan Leo: > might be dan Moose: > feels like a dan thing Leo: > very dan Moose: > what does it mean though Leo: > blood? Moose: > yeah obviously blood but like > why Leo: > because he thinks he's edgy Moose: > he doesn't think he's edgy. he supports wolves. Leo: > those aren't mutually exclusive There was a pause. The typing indicator appeared, disappeared, appeared again. Moose was composing and deleting, which meant he was thinking, which meant he was about to say something reasonable that Leo didn't want to hear. Moose: > what if it's not dan Leo: > it's dan Moose: > but what if it's not Leo: > then who Moose: > idk. that's the problem. Leo looked at the three blood drops again. In the dark, on his screen, they were oddly vivid—redder than emoji red, almost wet-looking, which was a stupid thing to think about an emoji but he thought it anyway. He locked his phone. The screen went black and he could see his own face reflected in it, faint and blue-tinged, looking exactly like someone in a horror film who was about to make a bad decision. He unlocked it. Leo: > it's dan Moose: > ok Leo: > we'll get him back tomorrow Moose: > how Leo: > idk yet. something good. something with blood. Moose: > please don't make this weird Leo: > it's already weird. we're just leaning into it. Moose: > that's not the reassurance you think it is Leo: > goodnight moose Moose: > goodnight. don't die. Leo: > no promises He put the phone on the charger. The cable was frayed near the plug and he had to wiggle it to get the charging icon, which was the kind of mundane annoyance that should have grounded him in reality but instead felt like a plot detail—the protagonist's unreliable technology, foreshadowing the moment when his phone dies at the worst possible time. He lay in the dark. The hall light was still on. He could hear his mum moving around downstairs—the clink of a mug, the soft thud of the kettle, the creak of the third stair from the bottom that she always stepped on because she never remembered it was there. She was probably making a candle. She made candles when she couldn't sleep, which meant the kitchen would smell like lavender and eucalyptus in the morning, and there'd be wax on the table, and she'd say "sorry about the mess, love" and he'd say "it's fine" and it would be fine. He should have gone to sleep. He should have closed his eyes and let the nothing-videos and the three blood drops dissolve into the usual soup of dreams that he never remembered. That's what a normal person would do. A normal person would think *weird text, probably a prank, go to sleep*. Leo was not a normal person. Leo was a person who had watched too many films and played too many games and read too many threads on Reddit about unexplained phenomena, and his brain was now running scenarios at a speed that made sleep impossible. What if it wasn't Dan? What if it was a wrong number? Some stranger, some random person, sending blood drops to the wrong phone. That happened. People misdialled. People sent things to the wrong contact. It was mundane. It was forgettable. But what if it wasn't? What if it was someone who knew him? Someone with his number, someone who'd chosen *him*, specifically, to receive three blood drops at midnight. That was targeted. That was personal. That was— *It's Dan.* But what if it wasn't Dan? He rolled over. The pillow was cool against his face. He could smell the faint ghost of the lavender from the kitchen, seeping up through the floorboards, and underneath it the older smell of the house itself—old carpet, old wood, the ghost of his dad's aftershave that had soaked into the hallway wallpaper years ago and never quite left. His phone buzzed again. He grabbed it. One new message, from the unknown number: ? Just a question mark. One character. No context. A response to nothing, because Leo hadn't sent anything. The unknown number was asking *him* a question, or maybe acknowledging his silence, or maybe just existing in the conversation like a ghost at a table, present but untouchable. Leo's thumb hovered over the keyboard. He could reply. He could type "who is this" and send it and wait. He could type something funny, something deflecting, something that turned the whole thing into a joke so he could control it. He could— He locked the phone. Put it face down on the bedside table. Pulled the duvet up to his chin like a child, which he was, technically, still, in the eyes of the law and his mum and the universe. The question mark sat in the dark, glowing faintly on a screen he couldn't see, in a message he hadn't answered, from a person he didn't know. Leo closed his eyes. Sleep came slowly, and when it came, it was thin and restless, full of half-formed images—canals in the dark, the sound of water, a door that was slightly open when it should have been closed. And through it all, persistent and inexplicable, the smell of lavender, rising up from the kitchen where his mum was making candles at midnight because she also couldn't sleep, because the Finch household was a place where no one slept and no one talked about why. In the morning, he'd tell himself it was nothing. He'd laugh about it with Moose. He'd come up with a plan to prank Dan that was elaborate and pointless and would probably backfire. But right now, in the dark, with the question mark still glowing and the lavender still rising and the hall light still on, Leo lay very still and listened to his own breathing and thought: *What if it's not Dan?*
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong opening chapter that demonstrates genuine craft and a clear authorial voice. Let me work through its qualities and weaknesses systematically. **Voice and Execution** The narrative voice is well-established from the first paragraph. The opening description of the scrolling-before-sleep state ("someone rating Greggs items, a cat falling off a shelf, a man explaining why the Roman Empire was actually just a vibes-based civilisation") is specific, observed, and funny without being try-hard. It earns its comedy through precision rather than performance. The voice is consistent throughout—Leo's film-literate, self-narrating interiority comes through naturally rather than being announced. **The Texting Exchange** This is the chapter's centrepiece and it largely succeeds. The rhythm of the exchange feels authentic—the way Moose replies instantly and then both of them pretend not to notice what that implies, the typing-indicator detail, the way "it's dan" becomes a mantra that Leo repeats to reassure himself. The line "he supports wolves" as a counterargument to edginess is genuinely funny and character-specific. The exchange does real work: it establishes the friendship dynamic, Leo's tendency to perform certainty, and Moose's quiet skepticism, all through naturalistic dialogue. **The Ending** The chapter plan called for ending "not on a joke but on the specific feeling of receiving something strange at midnight and not being able to shake it. One paragraph. Plain. No comedy." The model executes this well. The final section slows down appropriately, the prose becomes plainer, and the repetition of "What if it's not Dan?" works as a structural device. The detail about the dad's aftershave soaked into the wallpaper is the chapter's best single image—specific, emotionally loaded, and not overexplained. **The Unexpected Reply** The chapter adds a detail not in the plan: the unknown number sends a single "?" back, unprompted. This is a smart addition that escalates the unease and gives Leo (and the reader) something to sit with. It's a good instinct. **Weaknesses** The "reflected in the black screen, faint and blue-tinged, looking exactly like someone in a horror film who was about to make a bad decision" line is the chapter's most self-conscious moment. It's Leo narrating himself cinematically, which is in-character, but the execution is slightly on-the-nose—it tells us what Leo is doing (casting himself as a film protagonist) rather than letting us observe it. It's the one moment where the meta-awareness becomes a bit clunky. The final paragraph about the Finch household ("a place where no one slept and no one talked about why") is the chapter's weakest line. It's the one moment of genuine purple prose—it reaches for profundity and announces itself too loudly. The chapter would be stronger if it ended on the plain repetition of "What if it's not Dan?" without the editorial summary. The description of the lavender smell appearing twice (once when Karen is making candles, once in the dream sequence) is effective the first time and slightly overdone the second. The dream sequence itself ("canals in the dark, the sound of water, a door that was slightly open when it should have been closed") is competent foreshadowing but feels a little generic compared to the chapter's best moments. **Character Consistency** Leo is consistent with his profile—the film references, the self-narration, the performance of certainty masking genuine unease. The detail about the frayed charging cable becoming a "plot detail" in his mind is exactly right. Karen's presence (the clink of mug, the kettle, the third stair) is handled with appropriate lightness—she's there without being intrusive, and the detail about her making candles when she can't sleep adds texture without over-explaining. **Chapter Plan Adherence** The chapter follows its plan closely: the midnight text, the texting exchange with typos and rhythm, the decision that it's Dan, the vague revenge plan, and the ending on unease rather than punchline. The addition of the "?" reply from the unknown number is a smart deviation that serves the plan's goals. **Overall** This is confident, well-controlled writing that mostly avoids the traps of YA fiction—it doesn't over-explain, doesn't moralize, and trusts the reader. The comedy lands when it lands, and the chapter knows when to stop being funny. A few moments of self-consciousness and one genuinely weak closing line prevent it from being exceptional, but it's a strong foundation. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 2: The Revenge Dan Kowalski was sitting in the canteen, eating a sausage roll, and looking exactly like someone who had never sent a blood-drop emoji in his life. "You sent us a text last night," Leo said, sliding into the seat opposite. Moose dropped into the seat beside him with the cautious precision of a man sitting down on a bench he suspected was wet. "What?" Dan said, through sausage roll. "Last night. Just after midnight. Three blood drops. Don't play innocent." Dan chewed. Swallowed. Looked from Leo to Moose and back again with the calm, slightly confused expression of a golden retriever who'd been accused of tax fraud. "I didn't send you anything last night. I was asleep. Like a normal person." "We checked," Moose said. "The message came from an unknown number, but we thought—" "You thought I'd buy a burner phone to send you blood emojis?" Dan set down his sausage roll. He looked genuinely offended, which was inconvenient, because Leo had been counting on Dan finding it funny. Offended Dan was harder to manage than amused Dan. Offended Dan had *feelings*. "Not a burner phone," Leo said, though he hadn't ruled it out. "Maybe you used someone else's phone. Maybe—" "Leo." Dan's voice was flat. "Why would I send you blood emojis?" "For a laugh." "What's funny about blood emojis?" "I don't know yet. That's why it's a mystery." Dan looked at Moose again. "Is he always like this?" "More or less," Moose said. "Show me your phone," Leo said. Dan stared at him. "Just—show me. Let me see your sent messages." "This is insane." "Just show me." Dan sighed the sigh of a man who knows he's innocent but also knows that innocence is not a defence against his friends' paranoia. He unlocked his phone, opened his messages, and handed it over with the resigned air of someone submitting to a police search. Leo scrolled. He scrolled through Dan's sent messages with the intensity of a forensic analyst, examining timestamps, recipients, content. There were texts to his mum about dinner, texts to a girl named Anika that were mostly emojis (none of them blood), and a text to a group chat called "Wolves lol" that contained a single message: "we are so back." No blood drops. No unknown numbers. Nothing. Leo handed the phone back. "Huh," he said. "Huh," Dan repeated. "Can I have my sausage roll back?" "You never lost your sausage roll." "I lost my appetite. That's worse." The three of them sat in silence for a moment. Around them, the canteen hummed with the usual lunchtime chaos—someone shouting about a lost bag, the clatter of trays, the specific frequency of thirty teenagers all talking at the same volume. Through the windows, the sky was the colour of a wet flannel, which was the default colour of the sky in Wefton from October to April. "So if it wasn't Dan," Moose said carefully, "who was it?" "I don't know," Leo said. And then, because admitting uncertainty felt like failure: "But someone sent it. And someone's going to pay." "Pay how?" Dan asked. "I haven't figured that out yet." "Brilliant." The plan, when it came, was not brilliant. It was the opposite of brilliant. It was the kind of plan that a sixteen-year-old boy devises at lunchtime on three hours' sleep and a vague sense of injustice, and it had all the strategic coherence of a cat falling off a counter. They would send something back. Something scary. Something that would make the unknown number think twice before sending cryptic messages to strangers in the middle of the night. The question was: what? "A clown," Leo said. "A clown," Moose repeated. "Everyone's scared of clowns." "I'm not scared of clowns," Dan said. "You're scared of other things." "Like what?" "Irrelevance," Leo said, and Dan threw a chip at him. They settled on a photo. Leo had one saved on his phone—a still from a film he'd watched last Halloween, of a clown standing in a doorway, backlit, face in shadow. It was genuinely unsettling, in the way that any image can be unsettling if you stare at it long enough and you're already primed for weirdness. "Send it," Moose said, though his voice had the tone of a man watching someone step onto a rollercoaster he'd already decided not to ride. Leo sent it. They waited. The canteen cleared around them. The lunch bell rang. They didn't move. They sat at the table with their phones out, watching the screens with the focused intensity of bomb disposal experts, waiting for a response. Five minutes. Ten. The canteen was empty now except for a cleaner who was mopping around them with the pointed patience of someone who'd like them to leave. Then: a single character. A question mark. ? "That's it?" Leo said. "That's it," Moose confirmed. "Maybe they're scared." "Maybe they're confused. We sent them a clown." "Scared and confused. That's good." Then, five minutes later, while Leo was still composing a triumphant message about psychological warfare, a new message came through. Not text. An image. It loaded slowly—the canteen Wi-Fi was never good and was especially bad in the afternoons, when every phone in the building was fighting for bandwidth like starved dogs at a bowl. The image pixelated, sharpened, resolved. It was a photograph of their school. The front entrance, specifically—the concrete canopy, the automatic doors, the sign that read WEFTON HIGH in letters that were supposed to look modern but instead looked like a ransom note written by an architect. The photo had been taken from the car park, near the bike sheds, at an angle that meant the photographer had been standing approximately where Leo and Moose had been standing at lunch. Leo looked up from his phone. Through the canteen window, he could see the car park. The bike sheds. The exact spot. "When was this taken?" Moose asked. His voice was different now—quieter, tighter, the voice he used when his anxiety was spiking and he was trying very hard to sound normal. "Today," Leo said. "Look at the sky. That's today's sky." They stared at the photo. They stared at the car park. They stared at each other. "So," Moose said. "Someone was watching us." "Looks like it." "At lunch." "Yep." "While we were planning to send them a clown." "Also yep." Moose closed his eyes. He did this sometimes when the world was too much—just shut down the visual input and exist in the dark for a second, recalibrating. When he opened them, his face was calm in the way that a lake is calm before someone throws a rock into it. "Right," he said. "So we've gone from 'it's probably Dan being edgy' to 'someone was photographing us from the car park.' That's an escalation." "It's not an escalation. It's a response. We sent them a clown. They sent us context." "That's not context. That's a *threat*." "It's a photo of a school." "A photo of *our* school. Taken from *our* car park. On the same day we sent them a threatening image. That's not context, Leo. That's someone saying 'I know where you are.'" Leo wanted to argue. He wanted to say that Moose was overreacting, that it was probably just a coincidence, that the angle was wrong and the photographer could have been anyone. He wanted to say all of these things because saying them would make the situation feel manageable, and manageable was what he needed it to be. But the photo was still on his screen, and the car park was right there, and the sky was the same colour in both, and the angle was exactly right, and Leo was not stupid, even when he was pretending to be. "Okay," he said. "So it's not Dan." "It's not Dan." "And someone's watching us." "Someone took a photo of the school from where we were standing." "Which is basically the same thing." "It's not basically the same thing." " Moose. Focus." Moose focused. He took a breath. He pulled out his phone and opened his notes app, and Leo watched him create a new note titled "INVESTIGATION" in capital letters, and beneath it, the first entry: > Suspect 1: Unknown. > Method: burner phone. > Status: they know our location. > Confidence: n/a. "It's a start," Moose said. "It's a list with one entry." "Every list starts with one entry." "Not every list. Shopping lists start with—" "Leo." "Right. Yeah. One entry. Good start." They left the canteen. The cleaner watched them go with the expression of a woman who had seen sixteen-year-olds do stupider things than leave a canteen in slow motion, and who was tired of all of it. Walking back to class, Leo felt it—that buzz, that electric hum that comes when something shifts from "probably nothing" to "maybe something," when the world stops being a flat, grey surface and develops texture, and depth, and the faintest suggestion of a plot. This was interesting. This was, against all probability and common sense, actually interesting. He caught Moose's eye and grinned, and Moose didn't grin back, because Moose was thinking about the photo and the car park and the angle and the fact that someone had been standing where they'd been standing, watching them, and none of it was funny. But Leo was grinning. Because this was the bit in the film where it gets good.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 2 of this darkly comic YA fiction demonstrates considerable craft and largely delivers on its stated intentions. Let me examine it across the relevant dimensions. VOICE AND CHARACTERISATION The narrative voice is consistently Leo's—sarcastic, film-literate, performing. The internal logic of his character (treating everything as a film, using humour as deflection, missing the emotional register of those around him) is well-maintained. The line "This was the bit in the film where it gets good" as a closing beat is exactly right for his character and lands with the intended irony, since the reader can see what Leo cannot: that Moose is genuinely unsettled. Moose is rendered with precision. His anxiety manifesting as the "lake before someone throws a rock" stillness, his methodical notes app, his flat corrective dialogue—all consistent with the profile. The detail of him closing his eyes to recalibrate is a small, specific, earned character beat. Dan's "golden retriever accused of tax fraud" moment is funny and true to his profile: easy, uncomplicated, slightly hurt. The chapter plan called for Dan to be "genuinely confused, slightly hurt," and this is achieved, though the hurt is perhaps slightly underplayed—the "flatness" mentioned in the character profile's flavour dialogue doesn't quite land as heavily here as it does in the sample dialogue provided. DIALOGUE The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. It's sharp, interrupted, realistic, and funny without being wall-to-wall punchlines. "I lost my appetite. That's worse" is a good Dan line. The exchange about clowns and irrelevance moves quickly and feels like actual teenage boys. The Moose/Leo exchange about "context" vs "threat" is particularly well-handled—it does character work (Moose's precision, Leo's deflection) while also advancing the plot and building genuine unease. The dialogue earns its comedy without sacrificing the creeping tension. PLOT EXECUTION The chapter plan called for: confronting Dan (done), Dan being confused and slightly hurt (done), checking Dan's phone (done), hatching the revenge plan (done), sending the clown image (done), receiving the question mark (done), then the photo of the school (done). The chapter follows its plan faithfully and efficiently. The escalation from "probably Dan" to "someone was watching us" is well-paced. TONAL MANAGEMENT The chapter successfully maintains the darkly comic register while allowing the final beats to introduce genuine unease. The closing image—Leo grinning while Moose doesn't—is the chapter's best moment: it shows rather than tells the gap between them, and it earns its weight without being underlined. This is exactly what the writing plan called for. METAPHOR AND PROSE The metaphors are mostly functional and appropriate. "The calm, slightly confused expression of a golden retriever who'd been accused of tax fraud" is fresh and funny. "The sky was the colour of a wet flannel" is a good Wefton-specific detail. "The voice he used when his anxiety was spiking and he was trying very hard to sound normal" is tell-don't-show but forgivable in close-third YA. The "lake before someone throws a rock" metaphor is slightly overworked—it's a familiar construction—but it doesn't embarrass itself. There are a few moments of slightly over-explained comedy. "It was the kind of plan that a sixteen-year-old boy devises at lunchtime on three hours' sleep and a vague sense of injustice, and it had all the strategic coherence of a cat falling off a counter" is funny but slightly laboured—the "cat falling off a counter" simile is doing work that the surrounding prose has already done. The chapter occasionally tells us something is funny or chaotic rather than trusting the scene to demonstrate it. WEAKNESSES The cleaner appears twice as a functional comic prop ("the pointed patience of someone who'd like them to leave," "the expression of a woman who had seen sixteen-year-olds do stupider things") and while both lines are fine, the repetition slightly diminishes the second. The chapter plan mentioned the cleaner only implicitly, and using her twice as a comic beat feels like a small missed opportunity for something more specific. The "Moose. Focus." / "Right. Yeah." exchange is slightly mechanical—it's a reset button that the chapter uses to move past a moment of genuine tension rather than sitting in it. The plan called for Moose's anxiety to be real and physiological, and while it's present, it's slightly smoothed over by the dialogue rhythm. The chapter is very competent and occasionally excellent. It doesn't quite reach the level of the best British YA comic writing—there's no single line that would stop a reader cold—but it's consistently good, tonally controlled, and faithful to both the plan and the characters. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 3: The List Leo's bedroom smelled like old toast and the ghost of Karen's latest candle—"Sunday Morning," which smelled like linen and something floral that Leo couldn't identify and didn't want to ask about because then his mum would explain the difference between bergamot and neroli, and he'd have to stand there and pretend to care about essential oils for five minutes while his actual life was happening. Moose was sitting on the floor with his back against the bed, phone in hand, thumb moving with the methodical precision of a man filing a tax return. On the screen was his notes app, the "INVESTIGATION" note now expanded to fill half a page, colour-coded in a system that Leo found both impressive and mildly unhinged. "Right," Moose said. "Who do we know who has our numbers, knows where we go to school, and would think blood-drop emojis are an appropriate form of communication?" "Everyone in Year 11," Leo said. "More specific." Leo paced. His room was too small for pacing—three steps one way, three steps back—but he did it anyway because pacing made him feel like a detective in a film, and feeling like a detective was more fun than actually investigating, which involved sitting on floors and making lists. "Okay. Ellis Park." "The new kid?" "He wears black. He keeps to himself. He once drew a weird symbol in his maths book." "What kind of symbol?" "I don't know. A weird one." "It was a Rubik's cube, Leo. Priya told me. She sits next to him." "A Rubik's cube *could* be a sigil." "It's a toy. It's a children's toy shaped like a cube." "In the right context—" "There is no right context in which a Rubik's cube is a sigil." Leo stopped pacing. "Fine. What about Mrs. Barker?" "Mrs. Barker," Moose repeated, in the flat tone of a man who has been asked to consider something he has already considered and rejected. "She confiscated your phone." "In Year 9." "With *malice*." "She confiscated it because I was playing Subway Surfers in double maths. That's not malice. That's pedagogy." "She looked at me when she did it." "She looks at everyone. She has eyes." "She looked at me with *meaning*." "Leo." Moose looked up from his phone. "Mrs. Barker is forty-seven years old. She drives a Volvo. She has a cat named Bismarck. She is not sending us blood emojis." "You don't know that." "I know that she's a history teacher with a cat named Bismarck. That's enough." Leo sat down on the bed. The springs complained. Everything in this room was old—the bed, the desk, the curtains that his mum had bought from Dunelm in 2019 and which had faded from navy to a colour that was technically blue but spiritually grey. He picked at a thread on the duvet cover and thought about suspects. "Priya," he said. Moose stopped typing. "Priya." "She said my haircut was interesting." "When?" "Year 10." "Leo, that was a compliment." "It was *loaded*." "It was about your hair." "People say things about your hair when they can't say things about your character. It's a deflection." "Or it's a compliment about your hair." "She could be behind this." "Priya Mehta, who is in the top set for everything and volunteers at the Wefton Animal Shelter on weekends, is sending us blood-drop emojis because she said your haircut was interesting in Year 10." "When you say it like that—" "It sounds insane. Because it is." The door opened. Karen appeared in the gap, holding two mugs and wearing the expression of a woman who has brought offerings and hopes they will be accepted. "Tea," she said. "And I made those little breadsticks you like. The ones with the—" "Thanks, Mum." "—cheese on. They're in the kitchen. Just help yourselves." She hovered. She was good at hovering—there was a specific Karen hover that occupied the precise boundary between "I'm respecting your privacy" and "I would very much like to know what you're doing." Her eyes moved from Leo to Moose to the phone in Moose's hand and back again. "You two collaborating on something?" "Homework," Leo said. "History," Moose added, because Moose was a better liar than Leo, which was ironic because Leo was the one who thought of himself as the creative one. "History! Lovely. What period?" "The... recent one," Leo said. "Well, that's good. You know, your dad—" She stopped. The word "dad" had left her mouth before she could catch it, and now it was in the room, sitting on the bed between them like an unwanted guest. Karen's face did a complicated thing—apologetic, annoyed at herself, determined to push through. "—your dad always said history was important. Anyway. Breadsticks. Kitchen. Love you." She closed the door. The silence she left behind was the specific silence that follows a parent accidentally mentioning the other parent—loaded, uncomfortable, briefly acknowledged by everyone present before being deliberately ignored. "She's nice," Moose said, after a moment. "She's a lot," Leo said, but there was no venom in it. He loved his mum. He was embarrassed by her. These were not contradictory states; they were the twin engines of being sixteen. He got up and retrieved the breadsticks. They were, objectively, excellent—crispy, cheesy, the kind of snack that fills a hole without requiring commitment. He brought the plate back and set it between them, and they ate in the focused, wordless way of teenage boys encountering free food. "Right," Moose said, brushing crumbs from his phone. "I've got the list." He turned the screen so Leo could see. The note was now a proper document: **INVESTIGATION - SUSPECT LIST** > **Ellis Park** (new kid, black coat, "weird symbol" = Rubik's cube) > Confidence: LOW > Reason for suspicion: general vibe > Reason against: it's a Rubik's cube > **Mrs. Barker** (history teacher, confiscated phone) > Confidence: VERY LOW > Reason for suspicion: Leo's feelings > Reason against: she's a teacher with a cat > **Priya Mehta** (said haircut was "interesting") > Confidence: ZERO > Reason for suspicion: none > Reason against: all of them > **Unknown** (burner phone, knows our location) > Confidence: UNKNOWN > Reason for suspicion: they sent the text and took a photo > Reason against: we don't know who they are, which is the whole problem "Four suspects," Leo said. "Three of whom are innocent and one of whom we can't identify." "That's a start." "It's a list of people who haven't done anything." "Every investigation starts somewhere." "Most investigations start with evidence. We started with a vibe." Leo took a breadstick. Crunched it thoughtfully. "The unknown is the interesting one." "The unknown is the only one with actual evidence against them." "Exactly. So we focus on the unknown." "How? We don't know who they are." "We know they have a burner phone. We know they were at the school today. We know they responded to our clown with a question mark, which means they're either confused or unimpressed, and both of those are data points." Moose stared at him. "You just said 'data points' like you know what that means." "I know what it means." "You got a C in statistics." "A C is a pass." "A C is a polite fail." "The point is," Leo said, "we have more information than we think. The phone number. The location. The timing. It's not nothing." "It's not much." "It's enough to keep going." Moose looked at the list again. He scrolled up, scrolled down, as if the answer might be hiding in the whitespace between entries. Then he locked his phone and put it in his pocket, and the screen went dark, and the list disappeared, and they were just two boys in a bedroom with breadsticks and no answers. "Okay," Moose said. "We keep going." "Yeah?" "Yeah. But if we end up in a canal, I'm blaming you." "That's fair." They finished the breadsticks. Karen had made too many, as she always did, because Karen expressed love through overcatering, and the evidence of this—the plate, the crumbs, the faint smell of cheese—lingered in the room like a warm, slightly embarrassing hug. Outside, the sky darkened toward evening. The rain started again, because the rain always started again in Wefton, tapping against the window in a rhythm that was either soothing or maddening depending on your mood, and Leo's mood was neither soothing nor maddening but something in between—the restless, electric feeling of being on the edge of something, even if that something was probably nothing. His phone buzzed. He grabbed it. Not the unknown number. Not Moose. Not Dan. Just a notification from his mum's Etsy shop: "Grove & Glow: New Listing! 'Forest Bathing' - Cedar & Eucalyptus - £12.99." He put the phone down. "Tomorrow," he said. "Tomorrow," Moose agreed. And in the quiet of Leo's room, with the rain and the toast-smell and the ghost of neroli, the investigation rested. Not solved. Not abandoned. Just paused, like a film at intermission, waiting for the next act. The unknown number sat in Leo's phone like a stone in a shoe—present, uncomfortable, impossible to ignore. And on Moose's phone, the list grew, entry by entry, suspect by suspect, colour-coded and organized, a tiny monument to the belief that if you just arrange the data correctly, the world will make sense. It won't. But they're sixteen, and they don't know that yet.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong execution of the plan's intentions. The writing plan called for a chapter that was "mostly dialogue—the two of them in Leo's bedroom after school, throwing out names, arguing, getting distracted," with Karen popping in, and ending with Moose finding the burner phone detail. The chapter delivers all of this with considerable skill. **Dialogue:** The dialogue is the chapter's greatest strength. The exchange about Mrs. Barker ("She confiscated it with malice." / "She confiscated it because you were playing Subway Surfers in double maths. That's not pedagogy. That's pedagogy.") is genuinely funny and character-revealing simultaneously. The Priya exchange ("She said my haircut was interesting." / "When?" / "Year 10.") is perfectly timed and lands because it's played completely straight. The dialogue captures the specific rhythm of teenage boys arguing—the flat repetition, the escalating absurdity, the way Moose's deadpan functions as a comedic counterweight to Leo's performative theorizing. These are not adult ideas of how teenagers talk; they feel observed. **Character Consistency:** Both characters are rendered faithfully to their profiles. Leo's film-brain, his tendency to perform, his careless self-absorption are all present without being labored. Moose's methodical anxiety, his quiet moral authority, his flat humor are all intact. The suspect list in the notes app—color-coded, with "Reason against: Leo's feelings"—is a perfect Moose detail. Karen's appearance is handled with real delicacy: the accidental mention of "your dad," the hover, the overcatering as love language. These are not quirky props; they're people. **The Karen Moment:** The plan specified that Karen "pops in with smoothies" (the chapter changes this to tea and breadsticks, a minor deviation that actually improves things—breadsticks are funnier and more specific than smoothies). The accidental "your dad" moment is the chapter's most quietly effective beat. It's handled with restraint: "The word 'dad' had left her mouth before she could catch it, and now it was in the room, sitting on the bed between them like an unwanted guest." This is the chapter's one genuinely poetic moment, and it earns it. The follow-up—"She's nice." / "She's a lot."—is perfect: Leo's love and embarrassment compressed into two words. **Weaknesses:** The chapter's ending is where it slightly overreaches. The final paragraphs—"the restless, electric feeling of being on the edge of something, even if that something was probably nothing," and especially the closing lines "It won't. But they're sixteen, and they don't know that yet"—are the chapter's most amateurish moments. The narrator stepping back to editorialize ("It won't. But they're sixteen") breaks the close-third-person lock on Leo and announces its own irony in a way that undercuts the subtlety the rest of the chapter achieves. The plan explicitly warned against "explaining the joke," and this ending does exactly that. It's the kind of closing that a writer puts in because they're not sure the reader will get it, and the reader would have gotten it. The Etsy notification gag is a nice deflating beat, but the paragraph that follows it—"the restless, electric feeling of being on the edge of something"—is slightly purple and slightly redundant after the more effective "stone in a shoe" image. The "stone in a shoe" metaphor is functional but not particularly fresh. The "warm, slightly embarrassing hug" for the breadstick smell is better—more specific, more earned. **Plot:** The chapter doesn't advance the plot significantly, which is by design—the plan called for character work and comedy. The burner phone reveal is present but slightly underplayed; the plan suggested it should deepen the mystery, but here it's almost a footnote. This is a minor structural issue. **Overall:** This is confident, well-crafted YA fiction with a strong comic voice, genuine character work, and mostly invisible prose. The ending is its weakest element, and it's weak in a specific, correctable way: it tells us what to feel rather than trusting the scene to do the work. But the bulk of the chapter is very good—funny, specific, and alive. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 12 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 4: Suspect Zero Ellis Park lived above the Co-op. Leo knew this because he'd overheard someone say it in registration, and he'd stored it away in the part of his brain that labelled people as "information to be filed under potential threats," which was a section that had been mostly empty until this week and was now dangerously overactive. They followed him after school. This was not, Leo would later reflect, one of his better ideas. It wasn't even one of his mediocre ideas. It was an idea that existed in the negative space below mediocre—a void of judgment so complete that future Leo would look back on it with the kind of cringing, full-body shame usually reserved for remembering song lyrics you used to sing with conviction. But present Leo, the Leo of the moment, thought it was genius. "We're not following him," Leo whispered, from behind a bin at the edge of the school car park. "We're conducting discreet surveillance." "We're following him," Moose said, from behind the same bin, pressed against Leo's shoulder in a way that was both intimate and deeply uncomfortable. "And he's buying a birthday card." This was true. Ellis Park, suspect zero, the boy in the black coat who Leo had decided was responsible for the blood-drop emojis based on absolutely nothing, was currently standing in the Card Factory at the Wefton retail park, holding a birthday card with a cartoon dog on the front and reading the message inside with the concentrated expression of someone choosing a card that actually means something. They'd tailed him from school. It had taken forty minutes. Ellis had walked to the bus stop, waited eleven minutes (Moose counted), taken the bus two stops to the retail park, and entered the Card Factory with the purposeful stride of someone on a mission. Leo had narrated the entire journey in a hushed, breathless whisper that made everything sound more significant than it was. "He's heading north." "He's walking to the bus stop." "He's waiting for the bus. This is suspicious." "Waiting for a bus is not suspicious. It's public transport." "He's getting on the bus." "Because he's going somewhere." "But *where*?" "Probably somewhere normal." "Or somewhere sinister." "Leo, he's going to the retail park. The retail park has a Card Factory and a Costa and a Pets at Home. There's nothing sinister there." "There's a Smyths Toys." "What's sinister about Smyths Toys?" "I don't know yet. But I'm keeping an eye on it." Now they were crouched behind a metal bin at the edge of the retail park car park, watching Ellis through the Card Factory window, and Leo was starting to feel the first, faint stirrings of doubt. Not because the surveillance was going badly—it was going exactly as planned, in the sense that they were surveilling and Ellis was being surveilled—but because the reality of Ellis Park was not matching the profile Leo had constructed in his head. The profile: a mysterious new kid, possibly a goth, definitely sending blood emojis, potentially dangerous, almost certainly harbouring dark secrets. The reality: a sixteen-year-old boy buying a birthday card. Ellis came out of the Card Factory. He held the card carefully, both hands, as if it were something fragile. He walked to the bus stop and sat down on the bench. The bench was empty. The sky was the grey-wet colour that was Wefton's default, and the rain had started again—light, persistent, the kind that doesn't soak you but dampens everything, making the world feel slightly defeated. Ellis sat there. Alone. Holding a birthday card against his chest to keep it dry. And something shifted in Leo. It wasn't a dramatic shift—not a revelation, not an epiphany, not the moment where the soundtrack swells and the protagonist suddenly understands the meaning of life. It was smaller than that. Quieter. A flicker of recognition that passed through him like a draft under a door. He knew what that looked like. That specific posture—shoulders hunched, head slightly down, the careful stillness of a person who has learned to make themselves small in public spaces. He'd done it himself, at twelve, sitting on the kerb outside his dad's new place in Telford, waiting for a lift that was always twenty minutes late and sometimes didn't come at all. The birthday card was different from a kerb, but the loneliness was the same—the same colour, the same weight, the same quiet resignation of a person who is alone and knows it and is managing. Leo said nothing. Moose, beside him, was quiet too. Maybe he saw it. Maybe he just sensed the shift in Leo, the way you sense a change in air pressure before a storm. Either way, he didn't speak. They watched Ellis wait for the bus. It came after seven minutes. He got on. The bus pulled away, and the bench was empty, and the rain kept falling on the spot where he'd been sitting, and the whole thing was so profoundly ordinary that Leo felt something he couldn't name—a tightness in his chest that wasn't anxiety or excitement but something worse: shame. He'd followed a kid to a Card Factory. He'd crouched behind a bin. He'd built a narrative in which this kid—this specific, real kid with a birthday card and a black coat and a quiet way of sitting—was a villain, a suspect, a character in Leo's story. And the kid was just a kid. Just a boy buying a card for someone, sitting alone in the rain, going home to whatever was waiting for him above the Co-op. "Leo?" Moose's voice was careful. "You okay?" "Yeah," Leo said. His voice sounded wrong—too quiet, too flat. Not his voice at all. "Yeah, I'm fine." "Because you've gone weird." "I'm not weird. I'm thinking." "You're thinking in a weird way." "What does thinking in a weird way look like?" "Like that. Like you're doing now. With the face." Leo didn't answer. He was still looking at the empty bench. The rain was filling the space where Ellis had been sitting, puddling on the plastic seat, running off the edges in small, constant streams that caught the light from the streetlamp and held it for a second before letting it go. "We should go," he said. "Yeah," Moose agreed. They walked to the bus stop. They didn't speak on the bus. They didn't speak walking from the bus stop to Leo's street, where their paths diverged—Moose left, Leo right, the same split they'd made a thousand times before, the geometry of a friendship measured in which way you turn at the junction. At home, Karen was in the kitchen, making candles. The house smelled of cedar and something else—something warm and slightly sweet that Leo couldn't place. She looked up when he came in. "Alright, love? Want some toast?" "Yeah," he said. "Thanks." He went upstairs. He sat on his bed. He pulled out his phone and opened the conversation with Moose, and his thumb hovered over the keyboard for a long time before he typed: > maybe it's not ellis Moose replied instantly: > obviously And then, a moment later: > you alright? Leo looked at the message. He thought about typing "fine," which was what he always typed, which was the default response, the one that closed conversations and maintained distance and kept everything safely on the surface where it couldn't hurt anyone. He typed: > yeah. just tired. Moose: > ok. night. > night. He put the phone down. The room was dark. The rain was still falling, tapping against the window in the same rhythm it had been tapping all day, and the house smelled of cedar and the ghost of lavender, and downstairs his mum was humming something tuneless while the wax melted, and Leo lay on his bed and stared at the ceiling and felt the shame settle into him like rain into soil—slow, quiet, soaking everything. He should have felt stupid. He should have felt embarrassed. He'd followed an innocent kid to a Card Factory and watched him buy a birthday card, and the whole thing was ridiculous and he should be laughing about it. But he wasn't laughing. He was thinking about a boy on a bench, holding a card against the rain, and the specific, inarguable loneliness of that image, and how easy it was to turn a person into a suspect when you weren't looking at them properly. He closed his eyes. In the dark, the shame didn't go away. It just settled, becoming part of the room, part of the night, part of the quiet, steady rhythm of the rain on the glass. And somewhere in the space between waking and sleeping, Leo thought—not for the first time, but for the first time with clarity—that maybe the problem wasn't the mystery. Maybe the problem was the detective.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong piece of YA fiction that largely delivers on its plan and demonstrates genuine craft. Let me work through its qualities and weaknesses systematically. STRENGTHS: The chapter follows its plan closely and faithfully. The plan called for: following Ellis to the retail park, the Card Factory reveal, the bus stop moment of recognition, Leo's shame, and the closing text exchange ending with "obviously." All of these are present and executed with care. The chapter does exactly what it promised—it uses the surveillance comedy to set up a genuine moment of human recognition. The dialogue is one of the chapter's strongest elements. The whispered exchange during the surveillance ("He's heading north." / "He's walking to the bus stop.") is genuinely funny, rhythmically tight, and character-consistent. Moose's flat corrections land well. The comedy doesn't overstay its welcome—it runs for the right amount of time before the tonal shift. The text exchange at the end ("maybe it's not ellis" / "obviously") is perfectly calibrated: economical, character-true, and quietly tender without announcing itself. The Ellis characterisation is handled with real sensitivity. The chapter respects the plan's instruction that Ellis is "just a kid" and that casting him as a suspect "says more about them than it does about him." The birthday card detail is specific and earned. The image of him holding it against his chest to keep it dry is the kind of observed, precise detail that makes fiction feel real rather than constructed. The Leo interiority is well-managed. The plan called for Leo's inner voice to "sometimes falter" and for those moments to be "written plainly, without wit." The chapter delivers this: the shift from the comedic surveillance narration to the quiet, flat prose of the bus stop scene is handled with discipline. The writing doesn't announce the shift—it just happens, and the reader feels it. The Telford memory is well-integrated. It's brief, specific, and does real character work without being laboured. It connects Leo's recognition of Ellis's loneliness to his own experience of his father's departure without spelling out the connection. This is the story trusting its reader. The ending paragraph—"maybe the problem wasn't the mystery. Maybe the problem was the detective."—is the chapter's most explicit moment of thematic statement. It's slightly on-the-nose, but it's earned by everything that precedes it, and it's placed at the very end where such moments are most forgivable. It doesn't undercut the chapter's restraint elsewhere. WEAKNESSES: The rain is used as a metaphorical device several times, and while individually each instance works, cumulatively it becomes slightly heavy-handed. "The shame settle into him like rain into soil—slow, quiet, soaking everything" is the most explicit of these, and it's the chapter's weakest line—a simile that announces itself too loudly in a chapter that otherwise trusts implication. The plan specifically warned against "impressive prose" and this line strains slightly toward impressiveness. The "soundtrack swells" meta-reference ("not the moment where the soundtrack swells and the protagonist suddenly understands the meaning of life") is a bit self-conscious. It's consistent with Leo's film-literate voice, but in a moment that's supposed to be the voice going quiet, it briefly reasserts the performance register before dropping it. It's a small misstep. The final paragraph's "maybe the problem was the detective" is, as noted, slightly too neat—a thesis statement that the plan explicitly said to avoid. It's not egregious, but it's the one moment where the story tells us what to think rather than letting us think it. The chapter is slightly long for its plan's ~1000 word target, running closer to 1400-1500 words. This isn't a fatal flaw, but some of the middle section (the bus ride home, the walk) could be tighter. OVERALL: This is confident, disciplined YA fiction with genuine emotional intelligence. The comedy and the tenderness coexist without either undermining the other. The characters are consistent with their profiles. The chapter earns its quiet ending. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 5: The Pump House The text came at 9:47pm. A location pin, dropped into the conversation with the casual precision of a brick through a window. No words. No explanation. Just a map fragment showing a spot by the canal—the Victorian pump house, the one everyone knew about and no one went near, the one with the boarded windows and the roof that sagged like a tired horse. Moose's reply was immediate: > absolutely not Leo: > we have to go Moose: > we absolutely do not have to go Leo: > someone sent us a location. that's an invitation. Moose: > that's a lure. there's a difference. Leo: > come on Moose: > no Leo: > please Moose: > no Leo: > I'll buy you chips after A pause. The typing indicator appeared, vanished, appeared again. Moose was weighing the value of his personal safety against the value of free chips, and the chips were winning, because the chips were from Dave's and Dave's chips were the best in Wefton and possibly the Midlands, and Moose was a pragmatist. Moose: > dave's chips Leo: > dave's chips Moose: > large Leo: > large with curry sauce Moose: > fine. but if we die I'm haunting you first. They met at the canal bridge at half past ten. The night was cold—properly cold, the kind of cold that finds the gaps in your clothes and settles in, making itself at home. The canal was black and still, reflecting the streetlights in long, wobbly columns that looked like liquid gold but were just water and sodium and the optical illusion of being sixteen and standing somewhere you shouldn't be at half past ten on a school night. The pump house sat fifty metres along the towpath, squat and dark against the sky. It had been built in the 1880s to manage water levels on the canal, and it had been abandoned in the 1970s, and since then it had served as a shelter for pigeons, a canvas for graffiti artists, and a source of urban legends that were mostly untrue but persistent—the ghost of a lock-keeper who'd drowned, the sound of machinery that no longer existed, the door that opened by itself on windy nights. Leo had his phone torch on. The beam cut through the dark in a wobbly, unreliable cone that illuminated approximately three square feet of reality at a time—towpath, puddle, nettles, the concrete lip of the canal, which was closer than Leo liked. "Stay away from the edge," Moose said. "I am away from the edge." "You're not. You're one step from the edge." "I'm two steps." "You're one and a half." "Moose—" "I don't want to have to tell your mum you drowned investigating a text message. She'd cry. I'd cry. It would be a whole thing." They reached the pump house. Up close, it was larger than Leo remembered—two storeys of brick and iron, the windows boarded with plywood that was peeling and damp and covered in a layer of moss that looked like green velvet in the torchlight. The main door was a sheet of corrugated metal, slightly ajar, held in place by a padlock that was more rust than lock. "Someone's been here recently," Moose said, pointing. The ground around the door was muddy, and in the mud were footprints—small, adult-sized, pressed into the wet earth with the clear definition of shoes that had been here within the last day. "Could be anyone," Leo said, though his voice had lost its usual bounce. The pump house, in the dark, was not a film set. It was a real building, derelict and damp and smelling of rust and stagnant water, and the air around it was colder than the air on the towpath, as if the building was generating its own microclimate of discomfort. They went in. The interior was a single large room, the ceiling lost in shadow above them. Leo's torch swept across concrete floors, rusted machinery, the remains of a metal staircase that led to a mezzanine level that looked structurally unsound. The walls were covered in graffiti—tags, mostly, meaningless strings of letters in spray paint that had faded to pastels. The smell was complex: damp, rust, something organic that Leo didn't want to identify. "Hello?" Leo called out, and immediately felt stupid, because who calls out "hello" in an abandoned pump house at half past ten at night? Only people in films. Only people who think their lives have soundtracks. No answer. Just the drip of water somewhere unseen, steady and slow, like a clock made of liquid. They moved deeper. The torch beam found the machinery first—massive iron wheels and gears, frozen in position, the ghosts of Victorian engineering that had once controlled the flow of water through the canal. They were impressive in the way that dead things can be impressive—monumental, silent, proof that something important had once happened here, even if no one remembered what. Then the beam found the corner. It was tucked behind the base of the staircase, in a space that was sheltered from the main room by a half-wall of brick. In the torchlight, it looked like a nest—arranged, deliberate, a pocket of domesticity in the industrial decay. A sleeping bag. Dark blue, unzipped, the kind you buy from Go Outdoors for fifteen quid. On top of it, a pillow—a real pillow, not a bundled-up jacket, but an actual pillow with a pillowcase that had once been white and was now a colour Leo could only describe as "lived-in." Next to the sleeping bag: a tin of beans, unopened. A plastic spoon. A torch—the cheap kind, yellow, that you get from petrol stations. A phone charger, coiled neatly. And, propped against the brick wall at the exact angle of something displayed with care, a drawing. Leo crouched. He pointed the torch at the drawing and the light caught it and held it and wouldn't let go. It was a child's drawing of a house. Crayon, on paper that had been torn from an exercise book. The house had a red door and a yellow window and a chimney with smoke coming out of it, and in front of the house were two stick figures—one tall, one small—holding hands. Above the house, in wobbly child's handwriting, the words: **ME AND MUM** Leo stared at it. The torch shook slightly in his hand—not much, just enough to make the drawing wobble on the wall, the stick figures swaying as if they were alive, as if they were walking, as if they were holding hands and walking away from the drawing and into the dark pump house where someone had been sleeping on the floor with a fifteen-quid sleeping bag and a tin of beans and a drawing of a home that wasn't here anymore. He didn't say anything. Moose, behind him, didn't say anything. The silence was different now. It wasn't the comfortable silence of two friends who don't need to talk, or the charged silence of two boys in a scary place. It was the silence that comes when you've been playing a game and you suddenly realize it's not a game—that the things you've been treating as clues and suspects and plot points are actually lives, actually people, actually the specific, heartbreaking details of someone else's reality. "Leo." Moose's voice was very quiet. "We should go." "Yeah." "Someone's sleeping here." "Yeah." "We should tell someone." "Yeah." They backed out of the corner. They walked through the main room, past the rusted machinery and the graffiti and the drip of water, and out through the corrugated metal door, and onto the towpath, and the night air hit them like a wall of cold that was somehow warmer than the inside of the pump house. They walked along the canal in silence. The water was black and still. The streetlights cast their long, wobbly reflections. The towpath was muddy underfoot, and their shoes made sucking sounds with each step, and neither of them spoke, because there was nothing to say that wouldn't sound either too big or too small for what they'd seen. At the bridge, where the towpath met the road, Leo stopped. He looked at Moose. Moose looked at him. In the sodium light, Moose's face was pale and serious, the anxiety not spiking but settling, becoming something quieter and more permanent—a knowledge, a weight. "We'll tell someone," Leo said. "Who?" "I don't know. The school. The council. Someone." "When?" "Tomorrow." Moose nodded. Then he said, very quietly, almost inaudibly under the sound of the water and the distant traffic and the wind in the trees: "What if they're still there?" Leo didn't answer. He didn't have an answer. The question hung between them in the cold air, and the cold air held it, and the night held it, and the black canal reflected nothing, because the canal doesn't care about sixteen-year-old boys and their mysteries and their sudden, sickening realization that the world is bigger and sadder and more complicated than they'd allowed themselves to believe. They walked to Dave's. They ordered chips—large, with curry sauce—and sat in the fluorescent-lit warmth of the café, surrounded by the smell of vinegar and the sound of Sandra wiping down tables, and they ate without talking, and the chips were good and the curry sauce was hot and the café was bright and safe and completely unable to erase the image of a child's drawing of a house with a red door and a yellow window and two stick figures holding hands. After, walking home, Leo said: "We'll figure it out." And Moose said: "Okay." And neither of them believed it, but they said it anyway, because sometimes saying something is the only tool you have, even when the thing you're saying is a lie wrapped in the shape of a promise. At home, Karen was asleep. The house was dark and quiet and smelled of nothing. Leo went to his room and sat on his bed and looked at his phone and saw the location pin still sitting in the conversation, a little red dot on a little map, pointing at a place that was real, that was dark, that was cold, where someone was maybe sleeping right now, or maybe not, and he didn't know which was worse. He didn't sleep. Not for a long time. And when he did, he dreamed of stick figures walking through dark buildings, holding hands, looking for a door that was red and a window that was yellow and a chimney with smoke, and finding nothing but concrete and rust and the steady, patient drip of water in the dark. In the morning, he'd think about what to do. In the morning, he'd make a plan. But right now, in the dark, with the rain and the silence and the weight of something he couldn't carry and couldn't put down, Leo lay still and listened to the house breathe and thought about a drawing of a house that was just a drawing, and a home that was just a memory, and a boy who might be sleeping in a pump house by the canal, and he didn't know the boy's name, and he didn't know the boy's story, and he didn't know how to help, and the not-knowing was the worst part of all.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter represents one of the stronger pieces of YA dark comedy writing I've encountered in this format. Let me work through it systematically. **Strengths:** The opening texting exchange is genuinely excellent. The rhythm is perfect — the typing indicator appearing and vanishing, Moose's moral capitulation to chips, the negotiation over curry sauce. This is exactly the kind of connective tissue the plan called for, and it works because it's specific and true to how teenagers actually communicate. "Large with curry sauce" as the price of personal safety is a small comic masterpiece. The tonal shift is handled with real skill. The plan called for the chapter to slow down and become genuinely creepy for a stretch, and the writing delivers. The description of the pump house interior — "the smell was complex: damp, rust, something organic that Leo didn't want to identify" — is restrained and effective. The machinery described as "the ghosts of Victorian engineering" is one of the better metaphors in the piece, earned rather than decorative. The discovery of the sleeping bag and the child's drawing is the chapter's emotional core, and it lands. The drawing — "ME AND MUM" — is not subtle, but it doesn't need to be. The power is in the specificity: the torn exercise book paper, the plastic spoon, the coiled phone charger. These details feel observed rather than invented. The moment where Leo's torch makes the stick figures appear to sway is the chapter's best image, and it's earned by the restraint that precedes it. The silence after the discovery is handled correctly. The plan explicitly called for a moment that "the comedy does not immediately undercut," and the chapter respects this. The boys don't make jokes. They don't deflect. They just leave, and the weight stays with them. The return to Dave's is tonally perfect — the fluorescent warmth, Sandra wiping tables, the chips that are good but can't erase what they've seen. This is exactly the kind of earned darkness the plan described. **Weaknesses:** The final section of the chapter, from "At home, Karen was asleep" onward, begins to overwrite. The plan warned against "impressive prose" and noted that "the writing should be invisible when it's working." The dream sequence — "stick figures walking through dark buildings, holding hands, looking for a door that was red and a window that was yellow" — is the chapter's most significant misstep. It's the kind of image that announces its own significance, which undercuts it. The plan specifically said to trust the reader, and this doesn't. The penultimate paragraph — "the rain and the silence and the weight of something he couldn't carry and couldn't put down" — is the chapter's most purple moment. "A weight he couldn't carry and couldn't put down" is a cliché dressed up as profundity. Similarly, "the black canal reflects nothing, because the canal doesn't care about sixteen-year-old boys" is the chapter trying too hard to be poetic in a way that the plan explicitly warned against. The "lie wrapped in the shape of a promise" line is also slightly overwrought — it's the kind of formulation that sounds meaningful but is actually just a rhetorical flourish. The plan called for the chapter to end with Moose saying "We should tell someone" and Leo saying "Yeah" and them not doing it. The chapter follows this but then continues for several more paragraphs, diluting the impact of that quiet, damning exchange. The chapter would be stronger if it ended at Dave's, or at the bridge, rather than extending into Leo's sleepless night. **Character consistency:** Leo's voice is well-maintained — the "only people in films" self-awareness, the performance instinct faltering in the face of reality. Moose's pragmatism (chips winning over personal safety) and his quiet moral seriousness ("We should tell someone") are both consistent with his profile. The dynamic between them — Leo performing, Moose grounding — is present throughout. **Plan adherence:** The chapter follows its plan closely. The tonal shift is executed. The discovery of the sleeping bag changes the register from game to reality. The boys don't resolve it. They go home. The plan's specific beats — the sleeping bag, the drawing, the "We should tell someone" exchange — are all present. The chapter is slightly longer than the ~1000 word target, and the excess is mostly in the final section, which is also the weakest section. **Overall:** A chapter that largely succeeds at a difficult tonal task. The comedy-to-dread transition is handled with skill, the central discovery is genuinely affecting, and the restraint in the silence after the discovery is admirable. The chapter is let down by its final section, which overreaches and explains what should be left implicit. The plan's warning — "no thesis statement," "trust the reader" — is violated in the closing paragraphs. But the core of the chapter is strong, and the chips-for-personal-safety exchange alone is worth significant credit. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 8 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 6: The Dracula The next text arrived at 3:14pm, which was both oddly specific and deeply inconvenient because Leo was in the middle of double maths and his phone was in his bag and Mrs. Barker was circling the room with the predatory patience of a woman who knew exactly which students had devices on their person and was waiting for one to vibrate. It buzzed twice. Then stopped. Then buzzed again. Leo caught Moose's eye across the classroom. Moose gave a tiny, almost imperceptible shake of his head—the universal signal for "do not check your phone, she will confiscate it, and then where will we be." Leo responded with a tiny, almost imperceptible shrug—the universal signal for "I know, but I want to." The lesson ended at 3:30. Leo had his phone out before the bell finished ringing. The unknown number: > DRAMA STUDIO. 6PM. COME ALONE. Leo showed it to Moose in the corridor. Moose read it twice, his face cycling through a series of expressions—skepticism, concern, a brief flicker of something that might have been excitement before the anxiety smothered it. "'Come alone,'" Moose read aloud. "That's from a film." "Every line is from a film if you think about it hard enough." "No one says 'come alone' in real life. That's a screenwriter's line." "Maybe they're a screenwriter." "We're not being lured to the drama studio by a screenwriter." "We're not being lured at all. It's an invitation." "A summons." "Same thing." "It's really not."
