We're just browsing." *Internal monologue, Chapter 3 (fragment, as he's going down):* > Something hits the back of his head and the world tilts and there's a sound like a branch snapping and he thinks *that's my skull* and he thinks *that's not possible* and the floor comes up to meet him and the last thing he sees is the carpet and it smells like dog and he thinks *I don't want to die in a house that smells like dog.* --- # KIM PARKIN **Background:** Thirty-five. From a middle-class family in Columbia, Maryland—her dad was an accountant, her mom was a schoolteacher, and she grew up in a house where everything was fine and nothing was exciting and she spent her whole childhood waiting for her life to start. She went to Towson University, majored in communications, got a job in marketing at a healthcare company, met Greg at a friend's wedding, got married at twenty-seven, had Leo at twenty-eight. Her life is the life she was supposed to want. She loves Leo with a ferocity that surprises her every day. She loves Greg in the way that you love someone you've been with long enough that love becomes a choice you make every morning. She is not unhappy. She is also not entirely sure what happiness feels like. She volunteers at Leo's school, bakes for bake sales (her brownies are genuinely good), and is on a first-name basis with the baristas at the local Starbucks. She is a good person in the way that most people are good—not heroically, just consistently. **Personality:** Warm, scattered, slightly overwhelmed. She talks fast when she's nervous (which is often) and laughs at her own jokes (which are not always funny but are always delivered with such genuine enthusiasm that you laugh anyway). She is generous in a way that's almost aggressive—she will offer you food, drink, help, advice, whether you want it or not. She is also anxious in a way she doesn't fully acknowledge—she triple-checks the locks, she worries about Leo constantly, she Googles symptoms when anyone in the family has a cold. She is the kind of person who says "I'm fine" when she is not fine, not because she's lying but because she genuinely believes that if she says it enough times it'll become true. **Idiosyncrasies:** - She has a "wine o'clock" joke she makes every Friday at 5 PM that Greg finds annoying and Mara finds funny. - She always offers Mara a glass of wine before she leaves, and Mara always says "I'm working," and Kim always says "oh right, duh," and they do this every single time. - She keeps a family calendar on the fridge that is color-coded and also completely inaccurate because she never updates it. - She says "oh my god" approximately forty times per conversation. - She has a specific way of hugging that's slightly too tight and slightly too long, like she's trying to absorb you. - She calls Mara "the best" at least once per visit. "You're the best, seriously." She means it every time. **Flaws:** She avoids conflict to a degree that's damaging. She knows something is wrong with her marriage—Greg is distant, Greg is always working, Greg doesn't touch her the way he used to—but she buries it under busyness and brownies and color-coded calendars. She doesn't ask hard questions because she's afraid of the answers. She is complicit in her own unhappiness because confrontation terrifies her more than quiet desperation. **Physicality:** Five-five, soft in the way that mothers of young children are soft—she carries her weight in her hips and carries her stress in her shoulders. Brown hair in a messy bun that she redoes seventeen times a day. A round, open face that shows everything she's feeling because she has never learned to hide it. She wears yoga pants and an oversized sweater tonight because she's "just going to dinner" and "who cares." She moves like someone who's always running five minutes late—quick, slightly jerky, always reaching for something she forgot. **Motive:** She wants to go to dinner with her husband and come home to a happy child and a living babysitter. That's it. She doesn't know what's coming. She is blissfully, painfully unaware. **Relationships:** - **Leo:** Her whole heart. She once cried in the car after dropping him at kindergarten because "he looked so small" and she told no one. - **Greg:** She loves him and she's afraid of losing him and she doesn't know how to fix what's broken because she doesn't fully admit it's broken. - **Mara:** She genuinely likes Mara and is slightly in awe of her competence and calm. She thinks Mara has her life together. She is wrong about this, but it's a nice thought. **Voice Samples:** > "Okay so Leo's eaten, bedtime is 8:30, there's ice cream but only one scoop because last time he had two scoops he was literally bouncing off the walls—like literally, he was on the couch, bouncing—and Greg's at a work thing, he'll meet me at the restaurant, and oh my god I'm so late. You're the best, seriously. There's wine in the fridge if you want some. Oh wait, you're working. Duh." --- # GREG PARKIN **Background:** Thirty-eight. Grew up in Ellicott City. His father was a surgeon, his mother was a homemaker, and he grew up in a house where excellence was the baseline and anything less was a quiet disappointment. He went to Johns Hopkins for undergrad, got an MBA from Wharton, and now works as a "financial consultant" which means he manages money for people who have too much of it. He is successful by every metric that matters to the world and empty by every metric that matters to himself. He married Kim because she was warm and pretty and laughed at his jokes and seemed like the kind of woman who would make a house feel like a home, and she has, and he resents her for it because he doesn't know what he expected but it wasn't this. He loves Leo—he genuinely does—but he doesn't know how to show it because his father showed love through expectations and Greg is repeating the pattern without realizing it. **Personality:** Controlled. Polished. The kind of man who wears a suit well and shakes hands firmly and makes eye contact just long enough to be confident but not aggressive. He is charming in the way that successful men are charming—it's a skill, not a trait. Underneath, he is hollow. He knows this. He drinks scotch neat and reads the Wall Street Journal and goes to charity dinners and none of it means anything and he's too deep in to get out. He is not evil. He is weak. He is the kind of man who makes one bad decision and then makes a hundred more to cover the first one, and now he's standing in a kitchen full of blood in a house he can't afford in a life he doesn't recognize. **Idiosyncrasies:** - He checks his phone under the table at dinner. Always. - He pours scotch using a specific measuring device because his therapist told him to monitor his intake. He uses it twice and then free-pours the third. - He has a handshake that's firm and dry and practiced and means nothing. - He adjusts his cuffs when he's uncomfortable—a small, precise movement that Kim has learned means *I don't want to talk about this.* - He calls Mara "the sitter" not "Mara" and he doesn't notice he does this. - He has a golf handicap of 12 that he mentions at dinner parties because it's the only thing about himself he can discuss without lying. **Flaws:** He is morally compromised in ways he has justified to himself so many times that the justifications have replaced the truth. He manages money for people who shouldn't have that money. He doesn't ask where it comes from. He takes a percentage. He sleeps fine. (He doesn't sleep fine.) He is also emotionally unavailable in a way that's killing his marriage. He knows Kim is unhappy. He tells himself it's temporary. Everything is temporary if you wait long enough. **Physicality:** Six feet, lean, well-maintained in the way that men with gym memberships and personal shoppers are well-maintained. Dark hair with early gray at the temples that he considers distinguished. A face that's handsome in a forgettable way—pleasant, symmetrical, the kind of face you'd trust with your money. He wears a charcoal suit tonight because it's a "charity thing" and he looks good in charcoal. He moves like someone who has been taught how to move—controlled, efficient, no wasted motion. He doesn't slouch. He doesn't fidget. He is a man composed of deliberate choices, none of them his own. **Motive:** He wants to survive. Not in the physical sense (tonight) but in the existential sense. He wants to keep his life from collapsing. He is at the dinner tonight because he has to be—not because he cares about the charity but because the people he works for expect him to be seen. His real motive is maintaining the illusion that everything is fine. When he returns home and sees the blood in his kitchen, his first thought isn't *is my family okay*. His first thought is: *How much does she know?* **Relationships:** - **Leo:** Loves him. Doesn't know how to be with him. Reads to him in a voice that's slightly too formal, like he's presenting at a board meeting. Leo doesn't notice because Leo loves his dad and doesn't know there's another way to be read to. - **Kim:** He loves her the way you love furniture. She's part of the house. She's part of the plan. He hates himself for thinking this and then he doesn't because self-hatred requires feeling something and he's not sure he does anymore. - **Mara:** She is staff. He doesn't think about her beyond that. This is about to change. **Voice Samples:** > "The sitter's here? Good. Tell her the alarm code is 4-7-2-1. I'll be at the Chesapeake Room by 7. Don't wait up." (He will say this without looking up from his phone.) *Internal monologue, Chapter 7:* > He's standing in the kitchen and there's blood on the granite countertops that Kim picked out and he hates and the blood is drying and turning brown and he's thinking about the safe upstairs and whether it's been opened and whether the ledger is still there and he's not thinking about his son sleeping in the closet or the woman on the front steps or the dead man on his floor and he thinks *what have you done* and he doesn't know who he's asking. --- # Notes on Character Dynamics **Mara ↔ Leo:** This is the emotional core. Their relationship is the engine that drives every decision Mara makes. It must feel real, specific, and earned in Chapter 1 so that everything after has weight. **Mara ↔ Pete:** This is the dynamic that creates the most interesting tension. Pete is not a monster—he's a weak man who made bad choices. Mara can see this. She kills him anyway. That's the tragedy. **Mara ↔ Gary:** This is the primal dynamic—threat and response. Gary is the one whose escalation forces Mara's hand. He is the reason the line gets crossed. His downfall should feel *necessary* but not *glorious*. **Mara ↔ Greg:** This is the quiet horror. Greg is the real monster of the piece—not because he does anything violent but because he represents the system that creates the Petes and Garys of the world. His money is dirty. His house is a stage set. His family is an accessory. The final look between him and Mara should contain the entire story's moral weight. **Mara ↔ Kim:** This is the relationship that haunts. Kim is good. Kim doesn't know. Kim thanks Mara for saving her child. And Mara knows that Kim's husband is a criminal, and she can't say anything because Kim's world would collapse, and Mara has already collapsed enough worlds tonight. --- # Notes on Voice Each character's voice should be distinct not just in dialogue but in the *prose style* when we're in their head (which is mostly Mara's, but briefly Pete's and Gary's in their respective POV moments). Mara's internal voice is dry, medical, and darkly funny. Pete's is anxious, looping, running-on. Gary's is blunt, declarative, and stupid in the way that confident stupidity is stupid. Kim's would be fast and warm and slightly breathless. Greg's would be controlled and hollow. The voices should feel like different instruments in an orchestra, not variations on the voice of the author.

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 1: Psghetti Kim Parkin is late. She's always late. Mara can hear her through the front door—rummaging, swearing softly, the sound of keys dropping and being picked up again. Mara stands on the porch with her hands in her jacket pockets and waits. She's been waiting for four minutes. She doesn't mind. Waiting is easy. The door swings open. Kim is a blur of messy bun and yoga pants and a sweater that's too big for her. She's holding one earring and searching for the other. "Mara. Oh my god. You're the best. Seriously. I'm so sorry. Greg's already at the restaurant and I told him I'd be there by seven and it's—" She checks her phone. "Oh my god. Okay. Come in, come in." Mara steps inside. The house smells like the candles Kim buys from Target—vanilla, always vanilla—and something else underneath it, something warm and lived-in. Kid smell. The good kind. "Leo's already eaten," Kim says, dropping the earring into her purse and immediately forgetting about it. "Well, he had chicken nuggets because I couldn't find the—the thing, the—the recipe I was going to try, the one with the—oh my god, it doesn't matter. He ate. He's fine. Bedtime is 8:30, but if he negotiates—and he will negotiate—8:45 is the absolute latest. There's ice cream in the freezer but only one scoop because last time—" "He bounced off the walls," Mara says. "He literally bounced off the walls. Like, physically. Off the couch, off the walls. Greg thought it was funny. Greg wasn't the one trying to get him into the bath." Kim pauses. Takes a breath. Smiles. "You're the best, seriously. There's wine in the fridge if you want some." "I'm working." "Oh right. Duh." Kim laughs. She does this every time. Mara doesn't know if Kim forgets the exchange or if it's a bit. Either way, it's fine. "Okay. I'll be back by eleven. Call me if anything—actually, you never call me, you're amazing, I don't know why I said that. Okay. Bye. Love you. Not you—Leo. Love Leo. I mean, I love you too, but in a different—oh my god, I'm leaving." The door closes. Mara stands in the hallway and listens to Kim's car start, reverse too fast, and disappear down the street. Then the house is quiet. Mara takes off her jacket and hangs it on the hook by the door. She kicks off her shoes. She does a quick walk-through—not because she's paranoid, but because she's done this enough times that it's habit. Front door: locked. Back door: locked. Windows: closed. She notes the alarm panel by the garage door. She doesn't know the code and she didn't ask. Not her business. She finds Leo in the living room, sitting cross-legged on the carpet in front of the TV, which is off. He's holding General Bitey and having a conversation with him in the dinosaur voice—a low, growly version of his own voice that he thinks sounds terrifying. "General Bitey says we should have psghetti," Leo announces without looking up. "You already ate." "General Bitey didn't." Mara crouches down. "What does General Bitey want on his psghetti?" "Ketchup." "That's disgusting." "General Bitey says it's an emergency. He says if we don't have psghetti, the dinosaurs will be sad." "The dinosaurs have been extinct for sixty-five million years, Leo." Leo looks at her. His face is completely serious. "That's why they're sad." Mara makes the psghetti. Leo stirs because he insists on stirring and Mara lets him because it doesn't matter if the pasta is overcooked, and also because Leo stirs with the concentration of a man defusing a bomb—tongue out, brow furrowed, General Bitey perched on the counter watching. He tells her about a kid at school named Aiden who said dinosaurs aren't real. "Aiden sounds like an idiot," Mara says. Leo laughs so hard he almost falls off his step stool. The psghetti is overcooked. Leo puts ketchup on his. Mara eats hers plain and doesn't complain. After dinner, they watch one episode of a cartoon about a dog who solves mysteries, which Leo has seen fourteen times and still laughs at the same jokes. At 8:15, Mara says it's bedtime. Leo negotiates. He gets until 8:30. The bedtime story is about General Bitey fighting a dragon. Leo provides the plot. Mara provides the voices. General Bitey wins, obviously. Leo's eyes are closing before the dragon is defeated. Mara pulls the blanket up. Leo rolls onto his side, clutching the dinosaur. "General Bitey says goodnight," Leo mumbles. "Goodnight, General Bitey." "Mara?" "Yeah?" "You're the best babysitter ever." She sits with that for a moment. Lets it land somewhere in her chest where she keeps the things that matter. "Go to sleep, Leo." The house settles. Mara goes downstairs. She finds a glass, fills it with water from the tap, and sits on the couch in the living room. The room is lit by a single lamp. The TV is off. It's quiet in the way that suburban houses are quiet—not silence, exactly, but the hum of appliances and the distant sound of a neighbor's dog and the occasional car passing on the street outside. Mara pulls a paperback from her bag. Page 84. She's been on page 84 for six weeks. She reads the paragraph. She reads it again. She doesn't absorb any of it. She sets the book down. Drinks her water. Looks at the drawing on the fridge—Leo's horse with too many legs, done in purple crayon, with a sun that's smiling. Below it, the color-coded family calendar that Kim never updates. Below that, a magnet from Ocean City that says "The Beach Is Calling." Mara cracks her knuckles. Index, middle, ring, pinky. Left hand, then right. She doesn't know she does this. She picks up the book again. Page 84. She reads the same paragraph a third time. The words swim. She's tired. She's always tired. Not the kind of tired that sleep fixes—something deeper, something that lives in her bones. She closes her eyes for just a second. Just to rest them. The fridge hums. The dog outside barks twice and stops. The lamp casts a warm circle on the ceiling. Mara opens her eyes. She doesn't know if she slept. She picks up the glass of water and takes a sip. She looks at the clock on the microwave. 10:47. Kim will be home in thirteen minutes. Everything is fine. Everything is quiet. She turns back to page 84. The lights go out. Not a flicker. Not a brownout. A clean, surgical cut—one moment there's light and hum and the sound of the fridge, and the next there's nothing. Just dark and the sudden, total absence of sound. Mara sets the book down slowly. Her glass of water is still in her other hand. She doesn't move. She listens. The dark is absolute. No streetlight through the curtains—these are the blackout curtains Kim installed because Leo was waking with the sun. No glow from the microwave clock, no standby light on the TV. Just black. Mara's eyes adjust. Slowly. The room takes shape—the couch, the coffee table, the outline of the doorway to the kitchen. Moonlight is trying to get through the curtains but not really succeeding. It's enough to see shapes. Not enough to see faces. She should check the breaker. That's what a normal person would do. Go to the garage, find the panel, flip the switch. Maybe a fuse blew. Maybe the storm knocked something out—wait, there's no storm. There hasn't been a storm in two weeks. Mara doesn't go to the garage. Mara sits very still on the couch and holds her glass of water and listens. The quiet is wrong. The quiet that comes after a power outage is different from the quiet before. Before, there's always something—the fridge, the HVAC, the low hum of a house keeping itself alive. After, there's nothing. Just the house, breathing. And then—somewhere below her, in the kitchen, maybe at the back door—a sound. A click. The sound of a latch being turned. Mara knows that sound because she checked that lock herself four hours ago. She knows the weight of it, the specific resistance of the mechanism. She turned it and tested it and moved on. Someone is turning it back. Mara sets the glass of water on the coffee table. Carefully. Without a sound. Her hand is steady. Her heart is not racing. Not yet. It will. But right now, in this moment, her body is doing the thing it does—the ER thing—where everything goes very still and very slow and her brain starts cataloging exits, weapons, distances. She needs her phone. Her phone is in the kitchen. Charging on the counter by the microwave. She left her phone in the kitchen. *Okay.* She needs to get to Leo. Mara stands up from the couch. She doesn't make a sound. She moves toward the stairs, barefoot on the carpet, and begins to climb. Each step is deliberate. Each step is silent. Behind her, in the kitchen, she hears the back door open. She hears footsteps.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong opening chapter that largely succeeds at its stated goals. Let me examine it carefully across multiple dimensions. **Characterization and Voice** Mara is rendered with considerable skill. The chapter establishes her watchfulness, her competence, and her dry humor without ever announcing these traits. The knuckle-cracking detail ("She doesn't know she does this") is deployed exactly as planned—showing rather than telling. Her relationship with Leo is earned through specific, concrete moments: the psghetti negotiation, the dinosaur logic exchange, the bedtime story where Leo provides plot and Mara provides voices. These feel genuinely observed rather than manufactured. Leo is excellent. The "That's why they're sad" line is the best moment in the chapter—it's funny, it's specific, it's exactly the kind of earnest seven-year-old logic that makes a child feel real rather than cute. He doesn't feel like a prop or a movie kid. The General Bitey dynamic is handled with restraint; it's present but not overplayed. Kim's entrance is well-executed. Her voice sample from the plan is essentially reproduced in the text, which is both a strength (it's a good voice) and a slight weakness (it reads as slightly too polished, too "written"). The "I love you—not you, Leo—I mean, I love you too, but in a different—" moment is charming and rings true. **Prose Quality** The prose is clean and controlled. Short sentences are used effectively during the transition to tension at the end. The chapter avoids the purple prose trap almost entirely. There are a few moments where the writing reaches slightly—"the things that matter" after Leo's "best babysitter ever" line is a touch sentimental, though it's earned by the context. "The house, breathing" is a small but effective personification that doesn't overstay its welcome. The observation about the quiet being "wrong" after the power cut is genuinely good—it's specific and true (the absence of appliance hum is a real, disorienting thing) and it shows Mara's perceptual acuity without explaining it. **Pacing and Structure** The chapter follows its plan faithfully. The domestic warmth is established, the relationship is earned, and the transition to dread is handled with restraint. The power cut is a smart choice—it's not the back door opening that signals danger, it's the silence that precedes it. The chapter ends on exactly the right note: not a cliffhanger exactly, but a held breath. The "She left her phone in the kitchen" beat is slightly telegraphed—the plan explicitly notes this as a plot device, and the execution makes it feel slightly convenient. A reader might notice the setup. However, it's handled quickly enough that it doesn't linger. **Dialogue** The dialogue is one of the chapter's genuine strengths. Kim's rapid-fire monologue is pitch-perfect. The Leo exchanges are specific and funny without being precocious. The "That's why they're sad" line is the kind of thing that makes a reader stop and smile. The "You're the best babysitter ever" / "Go to sleep, Leo" exchange is quietly devastating in the right way—it doesn't announce its emotional weight. **Following the Plan** The chapter follows its plan closely and well. The plan called for "no ominous foreshadowing"—the chapter mostly honors this, though the power cut at the end is a fairly conventional horror-story device. The plan also called for the chapter to feel like "a long exhale—the last moment of peace before everything breaks," and it achieves this. The transition to dread at the end is perhaps slightly more extended than necessary (the paragraph about the quiet being "wrong" and the ER cataloging could be trimmed), but it's not egregious. **Weaknesses** The chapter's main weakness is that it's somewhat predictable in its structure. The warm domestic opening followed by the ominous ending is a well-worn template, and while it's executed well, it doesn't surprise. The power cut as a signal of intrusion is a genre convention. The "I left my phone in the kitchen" beat is a familiar thriller device. These aren't fatal flaws—the execution is strong enough to carry them—but they prevent the chapter from being truly surprising. The final line ("She hears footsteps") is slightly flat as a chapter-ender. The plan called for ending on "I left my phone downstairs" as a repeated internal thought, which would have been more distinctive. The actual ending is competent but generic. The observation that Mara "doesn't know if she slept" is a small but real weakness—it's a slightly lazy way to handle the time jump and feels like a shortcut. **Overall Assessment** This is a well-crafted opening chapter that establishes character, tone, and stakes with considerable skill. It's not a masterpiece—it's too conventional in its structure and too reliant on genre devices—but it's genuinely good work. The Leo-Mara relationship is the chapter's heart and it beats strongly. The prose is controlled and purposeful. The transition to dread is handled with more restraint than most genre fiction would manage. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 9 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 2: The Sound Two sets of footsteps. She can tell because one is heavier—a flat, deliberate tread—and the other is quicker, lighter, shuffling slightly. They're in the kitchen. She hears the dull clink of metal on granite—the sound of someone placing something on the counter. A flashlight? A gun? She doesn't wait to find out. Mara reaches the top of the stairs. Fourteen steps. She counted them once without meaning to and now she can't uncount them. She turns left toward Leo's room. The door is open. She can see the shape of his bed in the dark—a lump under a dinosaur-print comforter. General Bitey is a small silhouette against the pillow. She doesn't turn on the light. She doesn't call his name. She slides her hands under his small body—one under his shoulders, one under his thighs—and lifts. He's heavier than he looks, the way all kids are heavier than they look. He stirs. His mouth opens. "Shh," Mara whispers. Her lips are against his ear. "We're playing a game. The quiet game. Can you win?" Leo nods against her neck. His arms wrap around her. He's warm and sleep-heavy and smells like the lavender bath soap Kim uses. Mara holds him against her chest and moves back into the hallway. She could put him in the master bedroom closet—it's a big walk-in, accessible from the hallway, with a louvered door that has slats she can see through. It's the best option. She could also go downstairs and out the front door, but the front door has a deadbolt that clicks loudly and she'd have to cross the kitchen to get to the back. She'd have to pass them. The closet it is. She carries Leo across the hall. The master bedroom is dark. The bed is made—Kim makes the bed every morning, a habit Mara respects because Mara hasn't made her bed since 2019. She opens the closet door. It doesn't creak. Thank god. She sets Leo inside on the floor, between Kim's dresses and Greg's suits. He's awake now, his eyes wide and catching the faintest glimmer of light from somewhere. "Stay here," she whispers. "Stay quiet. General Bitey is in charge." Leo clutches the dinosaur. He nods. His eyes are huge. Mara closes the closet door. Not all the way—she leaves it cracked a quarter inch so he can breathe and so she can see the sliver of light from the hallway if someone opens the bedroom door. She stands in the dark bedroom and listens. Downstairs, the voices are clearer now. Not whispers. *Voices.* Normal volume, like they don't care who hears them. Like they already checked and confirmed the house is empty. "Bedroom's up here," one says. The lighter voice. Nervous energy. "There's gotta be watches, jewelry, whatever." "Relax," the other one says. Deeper. Slower. The heavy footsteps. "We got time. The guy said they're at some charity thing. Won't be back till midnight." *The guy said.* Not random. Not a coincidence. Someone told them the house would be empty. Mara files this. She files everything. It's what she does. She needs her phone. She needs to call 911. Her phone is downstairs on the kitchen counter, plugged into the wall charger, probably still at 23% because she forgot to plug it in last night and it charged all day and she meant to grab it before Kim left but Kim was talking about psghetti and the ice cream rule and Mara forgot. Of course she forgot. The stairs creak. They're coming up. Mara looks around the bedroom. The bed. The nightstands. A lamp with a heavy base—brass, maybe five pounds. She picks it up. Disconnects the cord from the wall silently. It's not a weapon. It's something to hold. She moves to the corner of the room, behind the door, where the angle is wrong for anyone entering to see her immediately. She presses her back against the wall. She grips the lamp base. Her knuckles go white. She doesn't think about what she'll do. She doesn't plan. Her body knows. Her body has always known—since she was twelve setting that broken finger, since she was twenty-two holding pressure on a femoral bleed while the attending barked orders, since every night in the ER when the ambulance doors slammed open and the gurney came through and her brain went cold and quiet and *ready*. The thinking part of her shuts down and the doing part takes over. It's not a superpower. It's a coping mechanism she never asked for. The first one enters the bedroom. The lighter one. She can see his silhouette against the window—he's thin, hunched, moving with the exaggerated care of someone who thinks they're being stealthy. He's wearing a hoodie. He has something in his hand—a phone flashlight, pointed at the floor, casting a pale cone of light across the carpet. He sweeps the room. Bed. Nightstands. Closet. He walks toward the closet. Mara's vision goes white. Not metaphorically. Her vision actually whites out for a second—a flash of something behind her eyes that isn't light but feels like it. Her hands grip the lamp base so hard her fingers ache. She doesn't breathe. She doesn't think. There is a man walking toward the closet where Leo is hiding and every cell in her body is screaming *no*. The man reaches for the closet door. Mara moves. She doesn't decide to. Her body decides for her. She steps out from behind the door and brings the lamp base down on the back of his head. It connects with a sound she'll never forget—not the clean crack of the movies but a dull, wet thud, like dropping a coconut on concrete. He makes a noise—a grunt, then nothing. His legs buckle. He drops the flashlight. It rolls across the carpet, casting wild shadows as it spins, and then settles, pointing at the wall. He's down. Not moving. Face-down on the bedroom carpet. Mara stands over him, breathing hard, holding the lamp base with both hands. There's something on her fingers. She doesn't look at it. A voice from the hallway. The deeper one. "Pete? The hell was that?" Silence. "Pete?" Mara drops the lamp base. It thuds softly on the carpet. She picks up the flashlight—turns it off immediately, plunging the room back into darkness. She listens. The heavy footsteps are on the stairs. Coming up. Faster now. He heard something. He knows something is wrong. Mara opens the closet door. Leo is sitting exactly where she left him, clutching General Bitey, his mouth clamped shut. She picks him up. He wraps around her like a vine. She moves to the back of the bedroom—there's a door here that leads to a small hallway, a servant's corridor from when the house was built that connects to the back stairs down to the kitchen. Kim showed it to her once, laughing: "It's like a secret passage. Leo loves it." Leo loves it. Mara uses it now. She carries Leo through the narrow hallway. It's dark and tight—barely wide enough for her shoulders—and she moves fast, one hand on the wall, the other holding Leo. Behind her, she hears the heavy one enter the bedroom. She hears him say "what the—" when he sees the body on the floor. She hears him call Pete's name again, louder now, and this time there's something under his voice that wasn't there before. Fear. Mara reaches the end of the servant's corridor. There's a door—a small, heavy door that opens onto the back stairs. She tries the handle. It's locked. There's a deadbolt—a simple slide bolt, no key needed. She slides it open with her thumb. The door opens. She carries Leo down the back stairs—seven steps, she counts them without meaning to—and into the kitchen. The kitchen. Her phone is here. She sees it on the counter, the screen dark, the charging cable attached. She grabs it. Unplugs it. The screen lights up—23%, as she predicted. She swipes. No signal. She tries anyway. Dials 911. The call doesn't connect. She tries again. Nothing. She looks at the phone. Looks at the signal bars. None. Zero. They cut the phone lines. The power and the phone lines. Not just burglars. They planned this. They knew what they were doing. Mara puts the phone in her back pocket. She holds Leo with one arm. She looks around the kitchen. The knife block is on the counter. Six knives. She doesn't take the biggest one—she takes the second biggest, an eight-inch chef's knife with a handle that fits her grip. She doesn't think about what she'll do with it. She thinks: *Leo. Safe. Now.* She carries Leo to the basement door. It's off the kitchen, behind the pantry. She opens it. The stairs go down into blackness. She flicks the light switch. Nothing—no power. She goes down anyway. The basement is finished—carpet, a couch, a TV that's dark. There's a water heater in the back corner, behind a half wall. She puts Leo behind it, in the space between the heater and the wall. It's tight. He fits. "Stay here," she says. Her voice is different now. Not gentle. Not the babysitter voice. Something else. Something she doesn't have a name for. "Don't come out unless you hear me say 'pizza time.' That's the code. Can you remember that?" Leo nods. His lip is trembling but he's not crying. He's being brave. He's seven years old and he's being brave and Mara's heart is breaking and she can't afford to break right now. "Pizza time," she repeats. "That's the only word. Okay?" "Okay," Leo whispers. Mara kisses the top of his head. She stands up. She closes the basement door. She locks it from the inside—a slide bolt, same as the servant's corridor. Then she goes back up the stairs, knife in hand, and enters the kitchen. She can hear them upstairs. The heavy one—Gary, she'll learn his name later—is moving around the bedroom, calling for Pete. His voice is getting louder, more panicked. He's not a professional. He's a man in a house with an unconscious partner and a woman who isn't where she's supposed to be. Mara stands in the dark kitchen. She grips the chef's knife. She fills the kettle and puts it on the gas stove. The click-click-click of the igniter is loud in the silence. Then the whoosh of the flame. She adjusts it to low. The water will take a few minutes. She turns off the kitchen lights. Wait—there are no lights. The power is out. She turns the flame down further, so the blue glow is barely visible. She grabs the heaviest frying pan from the rack above the island. Cast iron. Maybe eight pounds. She sets it on the counter next to the knife. She waits. The kettle begins to whisper.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter represents a strong execution of a tense, well-crafted thriller sequence. Let me examine it across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The prose is controlled and purposeful. The chapter opens in medias res with Mara already in motion, which is exactly right for the plan's intent to shift from domestic to dread. The sentence rhythm is genuinely skilled—short declaratives during action ("She doesn't turn on the light. She doesn't call his name."), longer constructions during the quieter observational moments. This isn't just a stylistic tic; it actually mirrors the character's psychological state. The specificity is excellent throughout. "He's heavier than he looks, the way all kids are heavier than they look" is a precise, earned observation. The detail about the lamp base—brass, maybe five pounds—feels like Mara's nurse-brain cataloguing weight and utility. The servant's corridor detail is well-planted and used organically. The 23% battery detail is a small, perfect touch of mundane realism. Mara's characterization is consistent with her profile. Her competence reads as procedural rather than superheroic—she counts stairs without meaning to, she files information ("The guy said. Not random."), she goes cold and functional under pressure. The line "It's not a superpower. It's a coping mechanism she never asked for" is one of the chapter's best—it earns its weight without being overwrought. The Leo scene in the closet is emotionally effective. "He's seven years old and he's being brave and Mara's heart is breaking and she can't afford to break right now" walks the line between sentiment and sentimentality without crossing it. The "pizza time" code word is a nice specific detail. WEAKNESSES: The chapter deviates significantly from the plan. The plan specified: Mara hears the back door, stays hidden, tries to get her phone (which she left in the kitchen), moves Leo to the master bedroom closet, and ends with Mara standing in the dark bedroom holding a glass of water, thinking about her phone. Instead, the chapter has Mara: using a lamp base to knock out Pete (who was supposed to be the wiry one, not the first one up the stairs), discovering the phones are cut, moving Leo to the basement (not the closet), and ending with Mara in the kitchen with a knife and a kettle. This is essentially Chapter 3 and 4 material compressed into Chapter 2, which disrupts the pacing architecture the plan carefully constructed. The plan specified Gary as the one who opens the closet (triggering Mara's white-out response in Chapter 3), but here it's Pete who gets knocked out first. The plan had Gary as the heavy footsteps/deeper voice, but the chapter seems to reverse this—the "lighter" one (Pete) comes upstairs first and gets knocked out, while the "heavier" one (Gary) is still downstairs. This is actually consistent with the profiles, but it means the chapter has already dispensed with one of the two antagonists before the story's midpoint. The "vision goes white" moment is used here, but the plan reserved this for Chapter 3 as the hinge moment. Using it here dilutes its impact. The discovery that "they cut the phone lines" and "they planned this" is an addition not in the plan and slightly strains credibility—it makes Pete and Gary more sophisticated than their profiles suggest. The dialogue is minimal but functional. Gary's "Pete? The hell was that?" is appropriately banal. The prose occasionally reaches for effect in ways that don't quite land: "Her vision goes white. Not metaphorically. Her vision actually whites out for a second—a flash of something behind her eyes that isn't light but feels like it." The over-explanation here ("Not metaphorically") is slightly clunky—it's the kind of thing a writer does when they don't trust the image. Overall, this is competent, engaging thriller writing with strong character consistency and good prose control, but it significantly departs from the chapter plan in ways that will create structural problems for subsequent chapters. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 14 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 14 Followed Chapter Plan: 7 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 3: First Contact The kettle whispers. Mara stands in the dark kitchen and listens to the house. Upstairs, Gary is calling Pete's name. The calls are getting shorter, less patient. There's a thud—something being knocked over. Then footsteps on the stairs. Coming down. Fast. Not trying to be quiet anymore. Mara moves. She picks up the kettle—it's not boiling yet, just hot—and puts it in the microwave. The microwave doesn't work without power but it's a metal box with a door that locks and right now it's a place to hide something hot. She picks up the frying pan and the knife and moves to the pantry. It's a walk-in pantry, deep, with shelves on both sides. She steps inside and pulls the door almost closed, leaving a crack wide enough to see the kitchen through. She waits. Gary comes through the doorway from the hall. She can see him in the thin moonlight from the kitchen window—a big man, enormous, filling the doorframe. He's breathing hard. He's got a flashlight in one hand, sweeping the room. The beam catches the counter, the fridge, the knife block. "Pete?" he calls again. Louder. Like volume will solve this. "Pete, if you're fucking with me—" He stops. He's looking at the knife block. Mara can see his silhouette turn toward it. He counts. She can see his head moving—one, two, three—he can tell a knife is missing. He knows she's armed. He reaches for one of the remaining knives. Pulls it out. The sound of steel sliding from the wooden slot is very loud. He sweeps the flashlight across the kitchen again. The beam passes the pantry door. Mara doesn't breathe. The beam moves on. "Okay," Gary says. He's talking to himself now. "Okay. Bitch, if you're in here, you better come out. I'm not gonna hurt you. We're just here for stuff. You come out, we take what we want, everyone goes home." Mara doesn't move. She knows this voice. She heard it a thousand times in the ER—the voice of men who come in with their victims, the calm-bro voice, the *hey, it's all good* voice that's always, always a lie. Gary moves through the kitchen. He opens the fridge—the light doesn't come on, no power—and slams it shut. He opens the pantry door. Mara is pressed against the back wall, behind the shelves, the frying pan raised. The flashlight beam sweeps inside—shelves, cans, boxes—and then moves out. He didn't see her. Or he saw something and decided it was nothing. Either way, he moves on. He goes to the basement door. Tries the handle. It's locked. He rattles it. Tries again. Then he kicks it. Once. Twice. The wood splinters but holds. He kicks it again. Mara can hear Leo whimper—faint, quickly stifled. Her grip tightens on the frying pan. Gary kicks the door again. It's giving. One more kick and it'll go. Mara steps out of the pantry. She doesn't announce herself. She doesn't warn him. She crosses the kitchen in three steps and brings the frying pan down on the back of his head with everything she has. The cast iron connects with a sound like a car door slamming. Gary staggers forward, hits the basement door with his shoulder, and turns around. He's dazed but not down. He's big—bigger than she thought—and the blow that would have dropped a normal man has just made him angry. He swings the knife at her. Wide, wild, the way people who don't know how to use knives use knives. Mara sidesteps—instinct, not training—and the blade catches her jacket, slicing through the nylon but not the skin. She can feel the cold air on her arm where the fabric parted. Gary swings again. This time she blocks with the frying pan—the knife clangs against the cast iron and the vibration runs up her arm and into her shoulder. She kicks him. Not a movie kick—a hard, ugly snap kick to the front of his knee. She doesn't know if it breaks but he buckles and his weight goes forward and she brings the frying pan down again. This time on his temple. The sound is different—sharper, more hollow. He goes sideways. Hits the counter. Slides down. He's not dead. She can see his chest moving. But he's not getting up. Not soon. There's a lump forming on the back of his skull the size of a golf ball and his eyes are half-open and unfocused. He's breathing in short, wet gasps. Mara stands over him. She's shaking. Not the fine tremor of adrenaline—that comes later. This is something else. This is her body catching up to what just happened. She hit a man in the head with a frying pan. Twice. She might have given him a subdural hematoma. She knows what a subdural hematoma looks like. She's seen them on CT scans, on the monitors, on the faces of people who didn't make it. She forces herself to move. She takes the knife from his hand—his fingers are loose, unresisting—and puts it in the knife block. She takes the frying pan and puts it on the counter. She checks his pupils with the flashlight. Left eye: reactive but sluggish. Right eye: the same. Not good. Not immediately fatal, but not good. She should call 911. She still can't. No signal. She could try the landline—is there a landline? She doesn't know. She hasn't seen one. Most people don't have landlines anymore. The Parkins probably don't. She picks up the chef's knife from the counter. She looks at Gary on the floor. He's a man. He came into this house to steal things and then he escalated—she could hear it in his voice, the shift from "we're just here for stuff" to something uglier. He kicked a locked door knowing there was a child behind it. He swung a knife at her. He's not a victim. He's a threat. But he's also unconscious and bleeding and she's a nurse and every instinct she has is screaming at her to help him. She doesn't help him. She goes to the basement door. The lock is broken from Gary's kicks but the door is still on its hinges. She opens it. Leo is behind the water heater, exactly where she left him, holding General Bitey against his chest. His face is wet—he's been crying, silently, the way children cry when they've been told to be quiet and are trying so hard to be good. "Pizza time," Mara whispers. Leo collapses into her arms. He's shaking. She holds him. She can feel his heart hammering against her chest—fast, fast, fast, the way a child's heart beats when they're terrified. "Are the bad men gone?" he whispers. "One of them is," Mara says. She doesn't know if it's true. She doesn't know if the other one—Pete, the skinny one—is still unconscious upstairs or if he's woken up and is coming down and she has to be ready. She carries Leo back upstairs. Not to the closet—somewhere better. The bathroom off the master bedroom. Small room, one door, no windows. A room you can lock from the inside. She sets Leo on the floor, next to the bathtub. She gives him General Bitey. "Stay here," she says. "Lock the door behind me. Don't open it for anyone. Not even if they say they're me. The only password is 'pizza time.' Can you do that?" Leo nods. He's trying so hard to be brave. His lip trembles but he holds it. Mara wants to stay. She wants to sit on the bathroom floor and hold him and not let go. But there's a man unconscious in the kitchen and another one somewhere in this house and she has to end this before Kim gets home, before Leo has to see any more of it. She leaves the bathroom. She hears the lock click behind her. Good boy. She goes back downstairs. Gary is still on the kitchen floor. Still breathing. She doesn't look at him. She goes to the servant's corridor—up the back stairs, through the narrow hallway, into the master bedroom. Pete is still face-down on the carpet where she left him. She checks him. Pulse: present but thready. Pupils: unequal. Not good. She drags him. It's harder than she thought—he's heavier than he looks, dead weight, and the carpet resists. She drags him to the bathroom adjacent to the master bedroom—the one without Leo—and leaves him on the floor. She closes the door. Two down. She doesn't know if there are more. She hasn't heard anyone else. The house is quiet again. The only sounds are Gary's wet breathing from downstairs and the distant hum of—wait, there's no hum. The power is still out. The silence is total. Mara stands in the master bedroom. Her hands are shaking now. The adrenaline crash. She looks at her palms. There's blood on them—Gary's blood, from the second blow, when the frying pan broke skin. She wipes her hands on her jeans. The blood smears. She wipes them again. She needs to think. She needs a plan. Two men are down, possibly with traumatic brain injuries, and she can't call for help and the power is out and there's a seven-year-old locked in a bathroom clutching a one-eyed dinosaur. She goes to the window. Pulls the curtain aside. The street outside is dark—no streetlights, either they cut the power to the whole block or the outage is wider. She can see a car parked across the street that wasn't there before—a dark sedan, lights off, engine off. Is that theirs? Are there more of them in the car? She watches the car. One minute. Two. No movement. No lights. Maybe it's just a parked car. Maybe it's not. She lets the curtain fall. She goes back downstairs. The kitchen is still. Gary is still on the floor. The kettle is still in the microwave where she put it. She opens the microwave door. The water is still hot—not boiling, but hot enough. She takes the kettle out. Sets it on the counter. She picks up the chef's knife. She waits.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] The chapter is titled "First Contact" but the plan called this Chapter 3 "The Line" — the moment of escalation where Mara crosses from hiding to fighting. The model has made a significant structural deviation: in the plan, Chapter 3 was supposed to feature Gary opening the closet where Leo is sleeping, triggering Mara's first violent response (the glass to the skull), and ending on a tableau with Pete. Instead, the model has collapsed and rearranged events considerably. Leo is in the basement (not the closet), Gary is kicking a basement door, Pete is already unconscious upstairs from a previous encounter that apparently happened off-page, and Mara fights Gary with a frying pan. The chapter also introduces elements planned for Chapter 4 (Mara arming herself in the kitchen, waiting in the dark) and Chapter 5 (the actual physical confrontation). This is a meaningful departure from the plan. That said, let's evaluate what's actually on the page. STRENGTHS: The prose is controlled and competent. The pacing is generally good — the chapter moves with urgency without becoming breathless or incoherent. The detail work is solid: Gary counting the missing knives from the knife block is a nice, specific touch that shows rather than tells his intelligence and Mara's tactical thinking. The "calm-bro voice" observation — Mara recognizing Gary's false reassurance from her ER experience — is exactly the kind of character-specific insight the plan called for. The "pizza time" password is genuinely touching and earns its emotional weight without being saccharine. The fight scene is appropriately ugly and unglamorous. The frying pan sounds like "a car door slamming," Gary doesn't go down cleanly, and Mara's nursing instincts warring with her survival instincts ("every instinct she has is screaming at her to help him / She doesn't help him") is well-executed and true to character. The moment with Leo behind the water heater — "his face was wet, he's been crying, silently, the way children cry when they've been told to be quiet and are trying so hard to be good" — is the chapter's emotional high point. It's specific, earned, and genuinely moving. WEAKNESSES: The chapter has a significant coherence problem: Pete is already unconscious upstairs, apparently from a prior encounter, but we haven't seen that encounter. The plan placed the glass-to-skull moment in Chapter 3, but here it seems to have happened off-page between chapters. This creates confusion — the reader is dropped into a situation where one antagonist is already neutralized without witnessing it. The chapter title "First Contact" is also misleading given that contact has apparently already occurred. The chapter deviates substantially from its own plan. The plan called for: Gary opening the closet where Leo sleeps, Mara hitting him with a glass, Pete's reaction, and ending on a tableau. None of this happens as described. The model has essentially written a different chapter — one that combines elements of Chapters 3, 4, and 5 from the plan. Some of the prose reaches for effect and doesn't quite land. "The silence is total" after establishing Gary's wet breathing is a minor contradiction. The observation about the parked car is introduced and then dropped without payoff, which feels like a loose thread rather than deliberate tension. The dialogue is minimal, which is appropriate for the chapter's tone, but Gary's "Okay. Bitch, if you're in here, you better come out" is functional rather than distinctive — it doesn't quite capture the specific menace the character profile promised. The chapter's ending — Mara waiting with a knife — is competent but anticlimactic. The plan's Chapter 3 ending (Mara standing over Gary, bleeding, staring at Pete in the dark) had more dramatic charge. The model occasionally tells rather than shows: "He's not a victim. He's a threat." is Mara explicitly labeling Gary's moral status rather than letting the reader feel it through action. Similarly, "She knows this voice" followed by an explanation of what the voice means is slightly over-explained. The chapter is coherent within itself but creates continuity problems with the plan's structure. The emotional beats are present but the chapter doesn't fully deliver on the plan's promise of "the moment everything changes" — that moment has apparently already happened. OVERALL: A competent, readable chapter with genuine strengths in character voice and specific detail, but undermined by structural deviation from the plan, a continuity gap regarding Pete, and some over-explanation that undercuts the "show don't tell" mandate the plan explicitly set for itself. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 13 Emotionally Engaging: 13 Compelling Plot: 11 Coherent: 10 Weak Dialogue: 7 Tell-Don't-Show: 7 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 9 Amateurish: 6 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 12 Characters Consistent with Profile: 13 Followed Chapter Plan: 5 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 13

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 4: The Terrain The waiting is the worst part. Not the fighting. Not the hitting or the screaming or the wet sound of a frying pan connecting with a skull. Those things happen fast—too fast to feel. The waiting is slow. The waiting is Mara standing in a dark kitchen with a knife in her hand, listening to a house that has been emptied of everything except threat. She doesn't know how long she stands there. Five minutes. Ten. The moonlight shifts on the floor—a slow, pale crawl across the tile. Gary breathes. That's the only sound. His breathing is ragged and wet, the sound of a man whose brain is swelling inside his skull. Mara knows this sound. She's heard it in the ER, in the trauma bay, on the monitors that beep slower and slower and then stop. She should do something. She should put him in the recovery position, check his airway, keep him stable until help arrives. She doesn't move. She thinks about Leo in the bathroom upstairs. She thinks about his lip trembling. She thinks about the way he said "are the bad men gone" in a voice so small it barely existed. She picks up the kettle and pours the hot water into the sink. She needs the kettle empty. She fills it with cold water from the tap and puts it back on the stove. She turns the gas on low—click-click-click, whoosh—and adjusts the flame until it's barely blue. The water will take time. Time is what she has. She goes through the kitchen drawers. Not the junk drawer—every house has one, full of dead batteries and rubber bands and takeout menus. She finds what she's looking for in the utility drawer: a roll of duct tape. Duct tape is the most useful thing in any house. She's seen it used to hold together a splint, to seal a sucking chest wound, to restrain a patient having a psychotic break. She tears off a long strip and sets it on the counter. She goes to the garage door. It's off the kitchen, through a small mudroom. She opens it quietly. The garage is dark—a two-car space, one side empty (Kim's spot, she drives a RAV4), one side with Greg's BMW. She doesn't care about the cars. She's looking for the breaker box. She finds it on the wall near the door to the house—a gray metal panel with a flip-up cover. She opens it. The main breaker is in the off position. Not tripped. *Switched off.* Deliberately. She flips it on. Nothing happens. She checks the individual breakers. All in the on position. She flips the main again. Nothing. She looks at the meter outside the garage—she can see it through the small window in the garage door. The digital display is dark. They cut the power at the pole. Not at the house. At the source. This is not a burglary. This is an operation. Mara files this. She goes back inside. The kettle is beginning to whisper again. She turns it off before it can whistle. She goes upstairs. The house is dark but her eyes have adjusted and she can see the shapes of things—the hallway, the doors, the family photos on the walls. Kim and Greg's wedding photo. Leo at age three, covered in cake. A school picture of Leo with the gap between his teeth and a smile that's trying so hard. Mara enters the master bedroom. She goes to the window and looks out at the street again. The dark sedan is still there. No movement. She watches it for a full minute. Nothing. She closes the curtain. She needs to know the house. Not the layout—she already knows the layout, she's been here four times. She needs to know the *resources*. What can she use? What can she weaponize? What are the chokepoints? The kitchen is the killbox. She already knows this. It's the most dangerous room in any house—glass, knives, fire, boiling water, heavy objects, hard surfaces. If she can lure them into the kitchen, she has the advantage. But she doesn't know how many there are. Two she's seen. Are there more? The car outside might mean a driver, a lookout. Three? Four? She doesn't know. She goes room by room. Not quickly—methodically. The way she used to triage in the ER. Primary survey: airway, breathing, circulation. Here, the primary survey is: exits, weapons, defensible positions. **Master bedroom:** One door, one window. Closet is too small to fight from. Bathroom off the bedroom—small, one door, no windows. Good for hiding. Bad for fighting. **Leo's room:** One door, one window. Nowhere to hide. Useless. **Guest room:** One door, one window. A heavy lamp like the one she already used. Nothing else. **Hallway:** Long, straight, no cover. If someone comes up the stairs, she can see them from the master bedroom doorway. If she has the knife, the hallway is a funnel. One at a time. That's good. **Kitchen:** Already assessed. The best room. Her territory now. **Living room:** Open plan, connected to the kitchen. The couch is too low to hide behind. The coffee table is glass—useless as a weapon but the shards could work. The TV is wall-mounted and can't be moved. The bookshelf is heavy but not useful. **Basement:** Locked. Leo is safe there. She won't use it as a fighting space. **Garage:** Tools. She didn't look carefully. She goes back. The garage is better than she thought. Greg has a workbench along the back wall—not because Greg is handy but because Greg buys things to look like the kind of man who has a workbench. On the wall above it: a pegboard with tools. Hammer. Screwdrivers. A hand saw. A pair of heavy-duty shears. A tire iron from the BMW's trunk. Mara takes the tire iron. It's heavy—maybe three pounds of solid steel, about eighteen inches long. She takes the shears too. They're industrial-grade, the kind used for cutting wire and sheet metal. The blades are six inches long and razor sharp. She carries them back to the kitchen. She sets the tire iron on the counter next to the knife. She puts the shears in the back of her waistband, against her spine. They're uncomfortable but accessible. She looks at the kitchen again. The kettle. The knife. The tire iron. The shears. The frying pan—still on the floor where she dropped it after hitting Gary. She picks it up. Wipes the handle. Sets it back on the counter. She opens the fridge. No light, but she can feel what's inside. She finds a carton of eggs, a block of cheese, a bottle of ketchup. She doesn't care about any of these. She finds what she's looking for: a squeeze bottle of mustard. Glass, heavy, with a pointed tip. She puts it on the counter. She opens the freezer. Frost bites her fingers. She finds a bag of frozen peas. She takes them out and sets them on the counter. Not as a weapon—as a cold pack. For later. For Leo, if he's hurt. For herself. For anyone. She goes to the bathroom off the master bedroom. Pete is still on the floor. She checks him again. Pulse: weaker. Pupils: still unequal. He's dying. Slowly, but dying. She could save him. She has the knowledge. She could stabilize him, keep him alive until the ambulance arrives. She doesn't save him. She closes the bathroom door. She goes back downstairs. The house is quiet. The moonlight has moved another inch across the kitchen floor. Gary is still breathing—slower now, more ragged. She doesn't look at him. She stands in the kitchen and she waits and she listens. The house breathes with her. Then—upstairs. A sound. Not footsteps. Something else. A creak, like weight shifting on a floorboard. Someone is up there. Someone who isn't Pete, because Pete is unconscious in the bathroom. Someone who isn't Gary, because Gary is on the kitchen floor. There's a third person. Mara's grip tightens on the knife. She backs against the counter, putting the island between her and the doorway to the hall. She waits. The creak comes again. Then nothing. Then the sound of something being opened—a drawer, maybe. A nightstand. They're searching the bedrooms. Looking for something. Not just random theft—searching. With purpose. Mara thinks about the sedan outside. She thinks about the power being cut at the pole. She thinks about the fact that they knew the Parkins would be out tonight. She waits. The footsteps come downstairs. Slow. Careful. Not the heavy, careless tread of Gary or the shuffling nervousness of Pete. These are measured steps. Controlled. Someone who knows what they're doing. A flashlight beam sweeps the hallway. It enters the kitchen. Catches Mara standing behind the island, knife in hand. The beam holds on her. Neither of them moves. Then a voice. Male. Calm. Not angry, not scared—*interested*. "Well," the voice says. "You're not who we expected." Mara doesn't answer. The flashlight lowers. She can see him now—medium height, lean, wearing dark clothes. He's holding the flashlight in his left hand. His right hand is at his side. She can't see if he's holding anything else. "Where's Gary?" he asks. Mara says nothing. "And Pete?" Nothing. The man takes a step into the kitchen. Mara shifts her weight forward. He stops. "You know," he says, "this was supposed to be easy. In and out. Nobody home. That's what we were told." "Who told you?" Mara says. Her voice sounds strange to her own ears—flat, toneless. The man smiles. She can see it in the flashlight glow—a thin, humorless curve. "Wouldn't you like to know." He takes another step. Mara raises the knife. The blade catches the light. "Careful with that," he says. "You might hurt someone." "That's the idea." The man studies her. She can feel him assessing—her stance, her grip, her eyes. He's not like the other two. He's calculating. He's deciding if she's a threat or a complication. "You killed them?" he asks. "They're alive." "For now." The words hang in the dark between them. Mara's hand is steady. Her heart is hammering but her hand is steady. "What do you want?" she asks. "Same thing we came for. I take it. I leave. You and the kid go back to watching TV." "The power's out." "I'll send someone to fix it." He's lying. She can hear it. Not in his voice—his voice is perfect—but in the pause before he speaks. The microsecond of calculation. He's telling her what she wants to hear. "Come and take it," Mara says. The man tilts his head. He's reappraising her. She can see the moment he decides she's not a complication—she's an obstacle. "Okay," he says softly. "Okay." He backs out of the kitchen. The flashlight beam retreats down the hallway. She hears his footsteps on the stairs—going up, not down. He's going to the bedrooms. Back to whatever he was looking for. Mara stands in the dark kitchen. Her hands are shaking again. She grips the knife harder to stop them. The kettle is cold on the stove. She doesn't turn the gas back on. Not yet. She waits.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] The chapter presents a significant and interesting deviation from the plan. The writing plan for Chapter 4 describes Mara taking control of the kitchen, waiting for Pete (who fled downstairs after Gary went down), and ending with Pete's silhouette appearing in the kitchen doorway. Instead, the model has introduced a third, unnamed antagonist—a calm, calculating professional who is clearly not Pete or Gary—and has restructured the entire chapter around this new character. This is a substantial departure from the plan, which explicitly stated "No hidden ledgers. No laundering. Just a suburban home on a Tuesday night" and "The story is about Mara and the child. The house is just a house. The parents are just parents." The introduction of a third operative who speaks of being "told" the family would be out, who cut the power at the pole, who is searching for something specific—this transforms the story into exactly the kind of thriller-with-hidden-secrets the plan explicitly rejected. This is the chapter's most significant flaw. The plan was carefully constructed to avoid the "ex-operative trope" and "moralizing twist about the family's secret," and the model has introduced both simultaneously. The mysterious third man who says "that's what we were told" and searches purposefully through bedrooms is a direct contradiction of the stated intent. That said, the prose itself is often quite strong. The tactical survey of the house—room by room, assessing exits, weapons, chokepoints—is well-executed and consistent with Mara's character as someone who thinks in systems. The ER triage parallel ("Primary survey: airway, breathing, circulation. Here, the primary survey is: exits, weapons, defensible positions") is one of the chapter's best moments, genuinely earning the character's background rather than explaining it. The detail work is good: the duct tape, the tire iron from Greg's performative workbench, the frozen peas as a cold pack rather than a weapon. The prose is clean and controlled. Short sentences during tension, longer ones during the methodical survey. The rhythm is appropriate. There's no purple prose to speak of, and the metaphors that appear are grounded rather than reaching. However, the chapter has a structural problem beyond the plan deviation: it's largely static. The plan called for "mostly silence and sound—the hum of the fridge, the kettle's whisper, Pete's footsteps, Mara's heartbeat" with "very little dialogue" and "almost no action until the last paragraph." The model delivers on the silence and preparation, but then introduces a lengthy dialogue exchange with the mysterious third man that feels tonally wrong. The third man's dialogue is too smooth, too movie-villain-adjacent ("Wouldn't you like to know"), and the exchange lacks the raw, unglamorous quality the plan promised. "You might hurt someone" / "That's the idea" is the kind of exchange that belongs in a different, more polished thriller—not this raw, ugly story. The decision to have Pete apparently die offscreen (Mara checks him, notes he's dying, closes the door) is handled with appropriate coldness, but it also means the chapter has quietly resolved a major plot thread without the visceral confrontation the plan promised for Chapter 5. The timeline is becoming confused. The detail about the power being cut at the pole is genuinely clever and creates dread, but it serves the new, unwanted conspiracy plot rather than the original story. The Greg subplot (the safe, the ledger) is being telegraphed in ways the plan explicitly forbade. The dialogue with the third man is the weakest element—it's functional but generic, and the third man himself is a stock character (the calm professional) that the plan was specifically designed to avoid. His line "Well. You're not who we expected" is a cliché. The chapter is competent, sometimes excellent in its tactical detail, but it has fundamentally betrayed the story's stated premise in service of a more conventional thriller plot. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 11 Emotionally Engaging: 10 Compelling Plot: 9 Coherent: 12 Weak Dialogue: 12 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 13 Amateurish: 6 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 9 Characters Consistent with Profile: 10 Followed Chapter Plan: 4 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 10

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 5: The Traps Spring The third man is in the master bedroom. Mara can hear him—moving methodically, opening drawers, closing them. He's searching for something specific. He's not hurrying. Mara uses the time. She moves through the kitchen fast. The duct tape comes off the counter—she tears three long strips and sticks them to the edge of the island where she can grab them. She takes the bag of frozen peas from the counter and drops it on the floor by the back door. Not a weapon. A marker. If someone comes through the back door, they'll step on it, and the crinkle of plastic will tell her where they are. She takes the cast iron frying pan and wedges it between the fridge and the wall, handle out, so she can grab it without looking. She takes the mustard bottle—glass, heavy—and puts it in the front pocket of her hoodie. She takes the kettle off the stove and pours the cold water out. She fills it halfway from the tap and puts it back on the gas. Low flame. It'll take five minutes to boil. She has five minutes. She goes to the living room. The coffee table is glass—tempered, but tempered glass still breaks into chunks that are sharp enough to cut. She doesn't break it. Instead, she moves it. Slides it across the carpet until it's in the doorway between the living room and the kitchen—not blocking the path, but making it narrower. Someone coming through fast will clip it. It's not a trap. It's a speed bump. Something to slow them down, make them stumble, give her an extra half-second. She goes to the front hallway. The coat closet. She opens it. Coats, boots, an umbrella. She takes the umbrella—a long, heavy golf umbrella with a metal tip. She opens it and wedges it in the closet doorway, handle toward the hall, so that if someone opens the closet door the umbrella will spring out at them. It won't hurt them. It'll startle them. Startled is good. She goes back to the kitchen. She opens the drawer next to the stove—the one that has the cooking utensils. She finds a meat thermometer. She pockets it. She finds a corkscrew. She pockets that too. She finds a pair of kitchen shears—the small, sharp kind used for cutting chicken bones. She puts them in her other pocket. She's arming herself with the contents of a suburban kitchen. She's aware of how absurd this is. She's aware that she's a twenty-six-year-old woman in pajama pants and a t-shirt, standing in a dead man's house (not dead, not yet, but dying) preparing for a fight with weapons she found in a drawer. In the ER, she had crash carts and intubation kits and a team of people who knew what they were doing. Here, she has a kettle and a tire iron and a bag of frozen peas. It'll have to do. The kettle begins to whisper. She turns the flame off. She picks up the kettle—the handle is warm but not burning. She moves to the base of the stairs and waits. Upstairs, the third man is still searching. She can hear him in the master bedroom now. He's moving faster—less careful, more frustrated. He's not finding what he wants. He opens the closet where Leo was hiding earlier—the door is still cracked from when Mara took him out—and she hears him push the hanging clothes aside. He goes into the master bathroom. Finds Pete on the floor. She hears him swear—not panicked, just angry. "Pete," he says. Shakes him. "Pete, wake up." Pete doesn't wake up. The man comes out of the bathroom. Mara can hear him standing in the hallway, thinking. She can almost see him—standing there, flashlight in hand, calculating his next move. He's alone now. Gary is downstairs, unconscious or dying. Pete is upstairs, unconscious or dying. He's the only one left. He calls out. His voice carries down the stairs, calm and clear. "I know you can hear me." Mara says nothing. "Pete's pupils are blown. That's a brain bleed. You did that." Mara says nothing. "You're not a babysitter." Mara says nothing. "Okay," he says. "Okay. I'm coming down." His footsteps on the stairs. Slow. Measured. The flashlight beam sweeps the stairwell, painting the walls in white light. Mara presses herself against the wall at the base of the stairs, in the shadow where the beam doesn't reach. She holds the kettle in one hand. The knife is in the other. She can feel the weight of the tire iron in her waistband. He reaches the bottom of the stairs. He pauses. The flashlight sweeps the hallway—left, right. He doesn't see her. She's three feet away, pressed into the corner where the stairs meet the wall, where the angle is wrong, where the shadow is deepest. He steps into the hallway. He's going toward the kitchen. He passes her. She can smell him—something sharp, cologne or deodorant, and underneath it, sweat. He's nervous. He's hiding it well, but he's nervous. Mara throws the hot water. Not at his face. At his back. The water hits him between the shoulder blades and soaks through his shirt and he makes a sound—a sharp, startled hiss—and stumbles forward. He drops the flashlight. It rolls across the floor and comes to rest pointing at the wall, casting a long, pale rectangle of light across the hallway. He spins around. He can't see her—the flashlight is behind him and the hallway is dark. He reaches for something at his waist. A gun. Mara sees the shape of it as he draws it—compact, black, the kind of gun that looks like a toy but isn't. She doesn't give him time to aim. She swings the kettle—still warm, still dripping—into his gun hand. Metal on bone. His fingers splay. The gun drops. It clatters on the hardwood floor and slides into the shadow under the stairs. He punches her. Not a wild swing like Gary—a tight, controlled right cross that catches her on the jaw. Her head snaps sideways. Her vision whites out again—that same flash—and she tastes copper. Her back hits the wall. He's on her. His hands are around her throat. Not squeezing—pinning. He's pressing her against the wall, his forearm across her collarbone, his face close to hers. "You should have stayed in the closet," he says. Mara brings her knee up into his groin. It's not elegant. It's not a technique. It's a seven-year-old's move, the first thing any person does when they're trapped and desperate. It works. His grip loosens. His face contorts. She drives her forehead into his nose. She hears the cartilage crunch—wet, ugly, like stepping on a snail. Blood splatters across her face—his blood, hot and metallic. He stumbles back. His hands are on his nose. Blood is pouring through his fingers. Mara drops the kettle. She pulls the tire iron from her waistband. She swings it one-handed—not at his head, she's not trying to kill him yet, she's trying to disable him—at his left arm. It connects with the forearm. She feels the bone give. Not break—*give*. The way a green branch gives before it snaps. He screams. A real scream—high, raw, the sound of a man who has never been broken before. He stumbles back into the kitchen. His foot hits the coffee table she moved earlier. He trips. He goes down. His back hits the kitchen island. He's sitting on the floor, cradling his broken arm, blood pouring from his nose, staring up at her. Mara stands over him. She's holding the tire iron in one hand and the knife in the other. She's breathing hard. Her jaw is throbbing where he hit her. Her forehead is wet with his blood. She can feel her heartbeat in her teeth. "Who sent you?" she asks. He laughs. It's a broken, bubbling laugh—the kind that comes with a broken nose and a shattered forearm. "You think I'm going to tell you?" "No," Mara says. "I don't." She hits him with the tire iron. Once. Across the temple. Not hard enough to kill—hard enough to end. His eyes roll back. He slumps against the island. His breathing slows. Mara drops the tire iron. It clangs on the tile floor. She stands there, shaking, her hands covered in blood, her jaw aching, her vision still swimming from the punch. The house is quiet. She goes to the gun under the stairs. She picks it up. It's a Glock 19—compact, reliable, the kind of gun that does exactly what it's designed to do. She checks the magazine. Full. She checks the chamber. Loaded. She's held guns before—in the ER, when patients came in with gunshot wounds, she'd help the police secure the weapons. She knows how they work. She doesn't like them. She puts the gun in her waistband. She goes to the kitchen. Gary is still on the floor. Still breathing—slower now, more ragged. She doesn't look at him. She goes to the third man. He's slumped against the island, unconscious. She checks his pupils. Reactive. He'll live. Probably. She takes the duct tape from the counter. She tapes his wrists behind his back. She tapes his ankles together. She tapes his mouth—not because she's worried he'll scream, but because she doesn't want to hear him. She goes to Gary. She tapes his wrists behind his back. She tapes his ankles. She tapes his mouth. He doesn't resist. He barely moves. She goes upstairs. Pete is still in the bathroom. She tapes his wrists. She tapes his ankles. She doesn't tape his mouth. She doesn't need to. He's not waking up. She goes back downstairs. She sits on the kitchen floor. Her back against the island. Her legs stretched out in front of her. She's still holding the knife. She doesn't remember picking it up. She sits there. She breathes. The house is quiet. The moonlight is pale on the floor. The kettle is on the stove, empty and cold.
